Topics/Household-Management-and-Domestic.title: Household Management and Domestic Service Occupation Profile
Topics/Household-Management-and-Domestic.content:
Household Management and Domestic Service Occupation Profile
Overview
The Household Management (“Keeping House”) and Domestic Service occupations represent a gender-specific occupational category in the Thorpe family research vault, with 6+ individuals documented almost exclusively as women across UK parishes and US settlements spanning 1841–1930. Census designations of “Keeping House” mask substantial agricultural and household labor by married women and daughters; the category reveals both census limitations in documenting women’s economic contributions and the gender-based occupational division characteristic of 19th-century industrial societies. Domestic service occupations document women as hired household workers, representing alternative economic pathways for unmarried or lower-status females.
Statistical Summary


















































MetricValueSignificanceTotal “Keeping House” documented6+Approximately 6% of 106 total population (heavily undercount)Total domestic service documented1–2Rarer occupational category; documented brieflyGender composition100% female (with one possible exception)Exclusively female occupational designationOccupational terminology”Keeping House,” “Domestic Service,” “Housekeeper”Variable census terminologyTemporal range1841–1930Consistent female occupational designation across centuryRelationship to farmersWives of farmers (majority); daughters co-residentIntegrated into farming householdsHousehold size5–9 membersLarge households reflecting extended family farming operationsSpousal occupational correlationMarried to farmers, laborers, artisansOccupational status reflects male head-of-household role
Documented Women in Household Management
Lincolnshire UK (1 documented)
Rebecca Blake (1779–1855)

Name variant: Rebecca Blake Lemmon (married name)
Birth/Death: 1779–1855 (age 76); Pennsylvania origin noted in some records
Occupation designation: Not explicitly listed in available census summaries; inferred from household role

Household Context:

Spouse: James Lemmon (1779–1854, farmer)
Children: Multiple children documented across 1841–1850 censuses
Household role: Wife of farmer; presumed household management responsibilities
Economic role: Undocumented in census; likely agricultural contribution (dairy, vegetable production, food preservation, textile production) not captured by “keeping house” designation

Implication: Rebecca Blake’s economic contributions to farming household uncounted in occupational census designation; represents typical census limitation in documenting women’s agricultural work
Ohio Settlement (2 documented)
Elizabeth Ault (b. ~1767, age 83 in 1850)

Census designation: No occupational entry in 1850 census (blank occupational field)
Household role: Wife of Andrew Ault (farmer, age 82)
Household context: Jefferson County, Island Creek Township; small household (3 members: Andrew, Elizabeth, Amy Palmer)
Economic inference: Wife of farmer; advanced age (83); presumably retired from active labor
Implication: Elderly wife of farmer; no occupational designation despite lifetime of household management

Hattie May Risden (1877–1967)

Census designation: “Keeping House” (1900 and later censuses)
Spouse: George B. Spicer (farmer)
Household context: Linn County, Iowa; large household with multiple children
Birth/Death: 1877–1967 (age 90); long-lived woman, productive household management across 50+ years
Occupational persistence: “Keeping House” designation continues across multiple census enumerations; consistent occupational label
Economic contribution: Farm household management; child-rearing; household production (textiles, food preparation, preservation)
Implication: Census captures Hattie May’s occupational role, but economic value unmeasured; “Keeping House” masks substantial household and agricultural labor

Relationship to Hattie May Risden:

Married George B. Spicer, unifying Risden and Spicer families
Multiple census documentation (1880, 1900) shows occupational consistency (“Keeping House”)
Long household management span (age 20s–80s estimated); 50+ year career in household management role

Iowa Settlement (1–2 documented)
Household Management in Spicer-Risden Agricultural Context:
Spicer Household Wives (1880–1930):

George B. Spicer household (1880, 1900): Wife listed in household; occupational designation not consistently recorded
Hattie May Risden: Primary documented household manager in Spicer household
Household role: Management of large household (8+ members in 1900); child-rearing; farm household coordination

Economic Contribution (Inferred):

Farm household production: dairy, eggs, vegetables, food preservation
Textile production: clothing, mending, blankets
Food preparation for large household
Child-rearing and education oversight
Cooperation with hired labor or extended family farm laborers

Census Invisibility: Most Spicer household female occupational designations blank or minimally documented; “Keeping House” designation inconsistent across censuses
Minnesota Settlement (1 documented)
Martha Eliza Lewis (1859–1923)

1900 Census Designation: No occupational entry listed (blank field)
1910 Census Designation: “None” (explicit designation indicating no occupational status)
1920 Census Designation: “house keeping” (explicit occupation entry)
Household context: Multiple Minnesota/South Dakota locations (Mower County MN 1900, Brown County SD 1910, Pine County MN 1920)
Spouse: Arthur Edwin Prior (farmer/laborer)
Household size: 7 members (1900), 4 members (1910), 3 members (1920); declining household size with age
Economic trajectory: Progressive household management over 60+ year lifespan; occupational designation inconsistent across censuses

Interpretation:

Martha Eliza’s occupational role varied in census designation (blank → “None” → “house keeping”)
Census inconsistency reflects ambiguity about unpaid household labor classification
Despite inconsistent designation, Martha Eliza’s household management role central to family economic organization

Occupational Patterns and Gender-Based Analysis
Pattern 1: Occupational Invisibility of Women’s Labor
Census Limitations:

Married women’s occupational designation frequently blank or minimal
“Keeping House” designation masks agricultural and productive contributions
Daughters listed as “At home” without occupational designation; household labor unrecognized
Census captures male occupational contributions; female occupational contributions undercounted or invisible

Specific Examples:

Elizabeth Ault: no occupational designation despite being farmer’s wife
Martha Eliza Lewis: variable designations (blank, “None,” “house keeping”) despite consistent household management role
Hattie May Risden: consistent “Keeping House” designation; economic value unmeasured

Implication: Thorpe family vault 1841–1930 documentation shows systematic gender bias in occupational census recording; women’s economic contributions substantially undercounted
Pattern 2: Household Management as Occupational Role
“Keeping House” Occupational Definition:

Census designation for adult women managing household
Includes: food preparation, textile production, child-rearing, household accounting, supervision of household labor
Prerequisite: marriage status (wives) or household head status (widows, elderly unmarried)
Occupational status tied to marital status; unmarried women without “Keeping House” designation

Documented Examples of “Keeping House”:

Hattie May Risden (1877–1967): “Keeping House” across multiple census enumerations
Martha Eliza Lewis (1920 census): “house keeping” designation in later life
Implicit in other women’s household roles: unrecorded economic contributions

Economic Interpretation:

“Keeping House” represents unpaid household labor within family economic system
No wage compensation; household management constitutes wife’s contribution to family economic unit
Large household size (5–9 members) indicates intensive household management labor

Pattern 3: Domestic Service as Alternative Occupational Pathway
Domestic Service Occupations (1–2 documented):

Documented: Women employed as household servants/domestic workers
Status: Hired labor, separate from family household
Economic situation: Employed women earning wages for household labor
Occupational pathway: Alternative to marriage/household management; available to unmarried or lower-status females

Contrast with “Keeping House”:

“Keeping House”: unpaid household labor within wife role
Domestic Service: paid household labor for non-family employer
Economic distinction: domestic service provides independent income; “keeping house” provides no direct wage

Pattern 4: Gender-Based Occupational Division
Male Occupational Dominance:

Male occupations documented extensively: farmer, farm laborer, miller, carpenter, chair maker, etc.
Multiple male occupations within single family (James Bellamy miller → sons various occupations)
Occupational transitions documented for males; career progression visible

Female Occupational Limitation:

Female occupations minimal: “Keeping House,” rare domestic service
No occupational diversity documented for women
Occupational transitions for women absent; “keeping house” lifelong designation
Age-related variation (Martha Eliza Lewis blank → “house keeping” with age) suggests occupational recognition tied to aging/widowhood

Implication: 19th-century industrial gender roles evident in occupational census: male occupational diversity and documentation; female occupational singularity and invisibility
Pattern 5: Household Agricultural Production (Invisible in Occupational Designation)
Agricultural Contributions (Inferred but Undocumented):

Farm households required female labor: dairy production, food preservation, vegetable cultivation
Textile production: spinning, weaving, sewing essential household activity
Food preparation: baking, cooking, food storage for large farm households
Child care and education: unpaid but essential household function

Evidence of Substantial Production:

Large household sizes (7–9 members) required intensive household management
Extended farm household roles suggest coordinated agricultural production
Spicer and Prior households documented with multiple farm laborers; household management of hired labor implied

Census Gap: None of these productive contributions captured in occupational designation; census records only “keeping house” as undifferentiated domestic role
Historical Context: Women’s Occupations in 19th-Century
UK Context (1841–1871)
Women’s Occupational Options:

Married women: “Keeping House” only occupational designation
Unmarried women: Domestic service, governess, textile work, agricultural labor (undercounted)
Census practice: Married women’s occupations subordinated to husband’s status
Economic reality: Wife’s household labor essential to farm family economic viability

Implication: UK agricultural economy dependent on women’s labor; occupational census invisible to economic understanding
US Context (1850–1900)
Occupational Opportunity Structure:

Married farm women: “Keeping House” designation (same as UK)
Unmarried women: Domestic service, textile work, teaching (alternative pathways)
Frontier economy: Women’s household production particularly valuable (food preservation, textiles, child-rearing)
Census practice: “Keeping House” continues to dominate; alternative occupations for unmarried women undocumented

Implication: US frontier agricultural economy similarly dependent on women’s household production; census documentation gap persists
Research Applications
For Genealogists

Marital status inference: “Keeping House” designation indicates married status (in absence of other occupational documentation)
Household role understanding: “Keeping House” provides clue to women’s functional household status
Economic status inference: Wives of farmers vs. laborers had different household economic circumstances
Occupational diversity: Minimal for women; “keeping house” lifelong designation typical

For Economic Historians

Household production documentation: “Keeping House” designation reveals unpaid household labor economy
Agricultural household organization: Large households indicate coordinated production with female management
Economic value measurement: “Keeping House” suggests substantial economic contribution; census undercounts female economic value
Gender-based occupational division: Male wage economy vs. female unpaid household economy clearly evident

For Social Historians

Gender role documentation: Census reveals industrial-era gender division (male wage earner, female household manager)
Women’s economic dependence: “Keeping House” designation tied to marriage; unmarried women different occupational pathways
Household labor intensity: Large farm households required extensive female labor
Aging and occupational status: Women’s occupational designation changed with age/life stage (Martha Eliza Lewis example)

For Women’s History

Occupational invisibility: Census systematic exclusion of female labor from occupational economy
Unpaid household labor: “Keeping House” captures unpaid work; no wage, no occupational autonomy
Labor intensity: Farm household management (5–9 member households) represents substantial occupational labor
Marital economic dependence: “Keeping House” occupational designation tied to wife status; no independent economic role

Research Gaps and Limitations
Critical Documentation Gaps

Household labor documentation: No records of specific tasks, hours, productivity
Wage or payment structure: No documentation of household management compensation (if any)
Economic value: No records of household production value (agricultural, textile, food)
Occupational alternatives: Unmarried women’s occupational pathways undercounted in census
Domestic service documentation: Only 1–2 individuals identified; broader domestic service occupations likely undercounted
Female agricultural labor: Direct agricultural work by women (dairy, vegetable production) hidden under “keeping house” designation

Census Limitations

Occupational designation inconsistency: “Keeping House,” blank, “None,” “house keeping” used variably
Female enumeration: Wives sometimes entirely absent from occupational column
Occupational ambiguity: Cannot distinguish active household managers from retired/elderly women
Age-related variations: Women’s occupational designation changed with age; cause unclear (retirement vs. census variation)

Next Steps for Research

Locate household production records (farm journals, diaries, account books) documenting female household production
Extract domestic service records (employment contracts, wage documentation) if available
Analyze household size correlation with female “keeping house” designation and household agricultural production
Research textile production documentation for farm households to estimate female productive contribution
Investigate unmarried women’s occupational pathways through complementary occupational records
Compare US vs. UK female occupational designations to identify regional variations
Study economic value of household production through agricultural economics literature
Analyze widows’ occupational transitions (women’s status changes after male death)

Cross-References
Related Occupational Pages

Farmer Occupation Profile — Male counterpart; farmer husband economic role
Farm Laborer Occupation Profile — Male agricultural labor role
Occupational Context and Economic Patterns — Aggregate occupational analysis

Related Geographic Pages

Linn County, Iowa — Spicer and Risden Settlement Center (Hattie May Risden household management)
Mower County, Minnesota — Lewis, Prior, and Palmer Settlement Center (Martha Eliza Lewis household context)
Sandusky County, Ohio — Lemmon and Ault Settlement Center (Rebecca Blake, Elizabeth Ault household management)

Related Family Pages

Lemmon Blake Thorpe Branch Summary (Rebecca Blake, female roles)
Spicer Risden Branch Summary (Hattie May Risden central household role)
Palmer Prior Lewis Branch Summary (Martha Eliza Lewis household management)
Ault Tallman Branch Summary (Elizabeth Ault, female household roles)

Individual Pages

Rebecca Blake — Farmer’s wife; household management
Elizabeth Ault — Aged farmer’s wife; household management
Hattie May Risden — Primary documented “keeping house” occupation
Martha Eliza Lewis — Household management with occupational designation variation

Source References

Census InDesign Summaries (occupational designation documentation)
Occupational Context and Economic Patterns (gender-based occupational division analysis)
Book Outprints Collection (burial records, biographical information)

Topics/Identity-Reconciliation-Matrix.title: Identity Reconciliation Matrix
Topics/Identity-Reconciliation-Matrix.content:
Identity Reconciliation Matrix
This page ranks the unresolved identity questions that can still be improved with the source material already present in the vault.
Reconciliation Diagram

graph TD
  A[High confidence<br/>image-level check needed]
  B[Medium confidence<br/>identity ambiguity]
  C[Low confidence<br/>branch-level only]

  A --> Ann[Ann Sorrell]
  A --> Eleanor[Eleanor Emblow]
  A --> Sarah[Sarah Kelly]
  A --> Susan[Susan Lewis]
  A --> MaryG[Mary Greenwood vs Mary Bergett]

  B --> Wynat[Wynat Lewis]
  C --> Uriah[Uriah Blake Thorpe vs<br/>Uriah Blake Lemmon]

The diagram is a visual index of the current queue. It does not imply any merge beyond what is written in the table below.
Ranking Key

High: Strong source-text support already exists; image-level confirmation is the main missing step.
Medium: The source text is useful but still leaves a meaningful ambiguity or competing interpretation.
Low: The current source set supports only a tentative note, not even a cautious merge candidate.

Matrix





















































ItemCurrent Evidence StrengthWhat the Vault Already SupportsNext Best StepAnn SorrellRESOLVED30-year census sequence (1841-1871) as wife of James Sorrell; age progression and household consistency confirmed; name variant (Sorrel/Sorrell) documented.✓ Identity confirmed via source text. Image verification pending (GSU rolls available).Eleanor EmblowRESOLVED30-year census sequence (1841-1871) as wife of Joseph Emblow; name variants (Eleanor/Elenor/Ellen) consolidated; duplicate “Eleanor Ireland” page deleted.✓ Identity confirmed and consolidated.Sarah KellyRESOLVED20-year census sequence (1841-1861) as household member, widow, and almshouse resident; consistent birthplace (Cambridgeshire, Whittlesey); clear family relationship to son James Kelly.✓ Identity confirmed via source text. Barton? was filename reference, not surname clue. Image verification pending (GSU/RG9 pieces available).Susan LewisRESOLVEDTwo-census sequence (1850-1860) in Wisconsin households with consistent family network (Wynat Lewis, Oliver Lewis); death inference confirmed (not in 1880 household).✓ Identity confirmed via source text. Image verification pending (M432/M653 rolls available). Death date 1860-1880 requires Wisconsin vital records.Mary GreenwoodVERIFIED SEPARATE1850 Iowa mortality schedule (age 38, died March) vs Mary Burgett (born 1835, died 1918). Age discrepancy (38 vs 15 in 1850) and different death dates confirm separate identities.✓ Correctly kept as separate pages. Both pages cross-reference and explain distinction.Wynat LewisRESOLVEDTwo-census sequence (1850-1860) with consistent household (wife Susan, children Oliver, Martha); age progression 68→79 matches 10-year span; pedigree timeline provides canonical form (Wynant Williamson Lewis).✓ Identity confirmed and canonical form established as Wynant Williamson Lewis. Image verification pending (M432/M653 rolls).Uriah Blake Thorpe vs Uriah Blake LemmonVERIFIED SEPARATEBirth dates 70 years apart (1808 vs 1878); death dates 1887 vs 1959; different states (Ohio vs Iowa); different burial sites (Clyde, OH vs Grand Mound, IA). Different generations, likely related through Blake/Lemmon/Thorpe family cluster.✓ Correctly kept as separate pages. Different generations confirmed via birth/death dates. Likely related but not same person.
Related Summary

Lemmon Blake Thorpe Branch Summary

Working Note
Use this matrix to choose the next session’s target:

If the goal is to reduce uncertainty fastest, start with the four High items.
If the goal is to reduce false merges fastest, work on Mary Greenwood and Mary Burgett together.
If the goal is to refine naming and canonical form, work on Wynat Lewis.

Topics/Jefferson-County-Ohio---Ault-Fami.filePath: Topics/Jefferson County Ohio - Ault Family Settlement.md
Topics/Jefferson-County-Ohio---Ault-Fami.title: Jefferson County, Ohio — Ault Family Settlement
Topics/Jefferson-County-Ohio---Ault-Fami.content:
Jefferson County, Ohio — Ault Family Settlement
Overview
Jefferson County, Ohio (eastern Ohio, Ohio River valley region) served as a primary settlement location for the Ault family during the 1800s. The Andrew Ault patriarch established farming operations here in advanced age, documented in the 1850 US census. Jefferson County represents a secondary Ohio settlement zone compared to Sandusky County, with documented Ault family presence spanning the 1800s.
Key Families and Individuals
Ault Family (Primary Settlement)

Andrew Ault (1769–1852, age 82 in 1850) — Patriarch; farmer; primary documented settler in Jefferson County
Elizabeth Ault (b. ~1767, age 83 in 1850) — Wife; long-lived spouse
Frederick Ault (1795–c.1857) — Son; farmer/tradesman; contemporary with Andrew in Ohio
Elizabeth Plomey Ault (1841–1926) — Granddaughter; later Ault-Tallman connection

Related Families

Amy Palmer (age 17 in 1850) — Household member; relationship to Andrew and Elizabeth unclear
Miller Mathias Tallman — Married Elizabeth Plomey Ault; Ault-Tallman union

1850 Census Snapshot
Andrew Ault Household — Jefferson County, Island Creek Township
Census Details: Series M432, Roll 699, Page 523





































NameRelationAgeSexOccupationBirthplaceAndrew AultHead82MFarmerNew YorkElizabeth AultWife83F—New YorkAmy PalmerRelated17F—Ohio
Household characteristics:

Both Andrew and Elizabeth in advanced age (early 80s); surviving into old age suggests health and economic stability
Farming primary occupation maintained into advanced age (82 years old)
Amy Palmer connection suggests intergenerational family ties; possibly granddaughter or niece
Isolated household (only 3 members) suggests limited extended family in immediate area

Economic implications:

Landownership implied by “farmer” designation in 1850
Ability to maintain household at advanced age suggests modest prosperity
Age of both suggests marriage ~1800–1810 (50-year marriage span)

Geographic Context
Location Details

Jefferson County seat: Steubenville, Ohio
Township context: Island Creek Township
Ohio River valley: Eastern Ohio border with Pennsylvania and West Virginia
Distance: ~120 miles southeast of Columbus; ~30 miles south of Lake Erie

Agricultural Suitability

Rolling hills and river valley; suitable for grain, livestock farming
Ohio River navigation provided commercial access in early 1800s
Secondary settlement zone (behind Ohio River valley primary settlements)
Less developed infrastructure compared to Sandusky County (northwest Ohio)

Ault Family Spread
Primary Location

1850 census: Andrew Ault documented in Jefferson County, Island Creek Township

Related Ault Locations (Ohio)

Sandusky County — Frederick Ault contemporary; possible family clustering
Ault-Tallman cluster — Broader family connections to Tallman line (Ohio settlement)

Settlement Timeline








































PeriodLocationKey PersonOccupationNotespre-1800New YorkAndrew Ault (b. 1769)UnknownBirth location implied1800–1840UnknownAndrew AultFarmer (implied)40-year gap in documentation1850Jefferson Co., OHAndrew Ault, age 82FarmerCensus documented1852Jefferson Co., OHAndrew Ault—Death (age 82–83)
Documentation gaps: Pre-1850 settlement date and location in Ohio unclear; intermediate locations unknown
Family Connections
Ault-Tallman Marriage Alliance

Elizabeth Plomey Ault (1841–1926, granddaughter of Andrew Ault) married Miller Mathias Tallman (1841–1921)
Unified Ault and Tallman families; later documented in Ault-Tallman cluster
Intergenerational family consolidation through marriage

Generational Succession

Andrew Ault (1769–1852, patriarch) → Frederick Ault (1795–c.1857, son, farmer) → Elizabeth Plomey Ault (1841–1926, granddaughter, married into Tallman)
Three-generation presence in Ohio (1800s–1920s)

Census and Economic Patterns
Occupational Context

Andrew Ault, age 82 in 1850: Farming primary occupation; maintained through advanced age
Implication: Economic independence and likely landownership
Comparison to Sandusky County: Similar age, occupation, and household structure as James Lemmon (also farmer, age 71 in 1850)

Household Composition

Small household: Only Andrew, Elizabeth, and Amy Palmer (3 people)
Implication: Limited extended family co-residence; contrast with larger Lemmon household (7 people)
Amy Palmer relationship: Unclear; possibly granddaughter or niece; Ohio birthplace suggests local-born relative

Research Implications
Strengths

Advanced age documentation: 82-year-old farmer provides rare late-life census snapshot
Occupational clarity: Explicit farming designation confirms economic role
Long-lived couple: Both ~1767 births, documented together age 83 (50+ year marriage)

Research Gaps

Pre-1850 Ohio settlement: Andrew Ault’s arrival date and initial settlement location unknown
Relationship clarification: Frederick Ault’s connection to Andrew (father/brother?) unclear
Amy Palmer identity: Her relationship to Andrew/Elizabeth unknown; possible granddaughter
Post-1850 settlement: No 1860+ documentation for Andrew (death implied 1852); Frederick Ault’s later settlement unclear
Land records: Farm location, acreage, land patents not documented

Next Steps for County Research

Locate 1850 Jefferson County, Ohio land records for Andrew Ault (property ownership confirmation)
Extract Ohio mortality/death records (1852) for Andrew Ault (confirm death date and location)
Research Island Creek Township records (1800–1860) for Ault family references
Locate Steubenville area church records for Ault family events (unlikely, given advanced age)
Clarify Frederick Ault relationship to Andrew Ault (investigate Ault family patriarch lines)
Investigate Amy Palmer identity (birth records, family relationships)

Cross-References
Related Geographic Pages

Sandusky County, Ohio — Lemmon Ault Settlement (primary Ohio Lemmon/Ault settlement)
American Settlement and Migration Timeline (Ohio settlement context)

Related Family Pages

Ault Tallman Branch Summary (primary family cluster)
Andrew Ault — Patriarch documentation
Frederick Ault — Farmer; related line

Individual Pages

Elizabeth Ault (b. ~1767) — Wife
Frederick Ault (1795–c.1857) — Son
Elizabeth Plomey Ault (1841–1926) — Granddaughter; Tallman connection

Source References

Census InDesign Summaries (1850 census details)
Thorpe Pedigree Timeline (Ault-Tallman connection)

Topics/Lemmon-Blake-Thorpe-Branch-Summar.title: Lemmon Blake Thorpe Branch Summary
Topics/Lemmon-Blake-Thorpe-Branch-Summar.content:
Lemmon, Blake, and Thorpe Branch Summary
This is the core Thorpe-connected branch hub. It explains why the Lemmon, Blake, and Thorpe pages are linked in the vault while keeping the evidence boundaries clear: the compiled pedigree timeline places these families in the same branch context, but individual relationships still need record-level confirmation in places.
Branch Diagram

graph TD
  HM["Hugh Lemmon<br/>1754-1824"]
  JL["James Lemmon<br/>1779-1854"]
  RB["Rebecca Blake<br/>1779-1855"]
  UBL["Uriah Blake Lemmon<br/>1808-1887"]
  SAL["Sarah Annett Lemmon<br/>1841-1886"]
  UBT["Uriah Blake Thorpe<br/>1878-1959"]
  HMR["Hattie May Risden<br/>1877-1967"]

  HM --> JL
  JL --- RB
  JL --> UBL
  RB --> UBL
  UBL --> SAL
  SAL --> UBT
  SAL --> HMR

This diagram is a visitor guide, not a standalone proof. It summarizes relationships and branch placement already discussed in the linked pages and in the extracted Thorpe pedigree timeline.
Start With These People

Uriah Blake Thorpe - later Iowa Thorpe identity, verified as separate from Uriah Blake Lemmon.
Uriah Blake Lemmon - earlier Ohio Lemmon identity in the same compiled branch context.
James Lemmon and Rebecca Blake - the Lemmon/Blake couple anchoring the older branch layer.
Sarah Annett Lemmon - visible bridge from Lemmon naming into later Thorpe household naming.
Hattie May Risden - descendant-line profile that connects the Lemmon/Thorpe context into the Spicer-Risden family story.

What We Know

The extracted Thorpe pedigree timeline places Uriah Blake Thorpe, Uriah Blake Lemmon, James Lemmon, Rebecca Blake, and Sarah Annett Lemmon in a shared branch context.
Uriah Blake Thorpe and Uriah Blake Lemmon are verified as separate people: they differ by about 70 years in birth date and have different death and burial contexts.
The Blake surname is part of the Lemmon ancestral context through Rebecca Blake, not a reason to merge the two Uriah Blake identities.
Sarah Annett Lemmon is the most useful bridge page for understanding how this branch connects forward into the later Thorpe/Risden/Spicer cluster.

What Remains Uncertain

Some early-generation placements still come from the compiled pedigree timeline and need image-level or record-level verification.
The exact relationship path between all Lemmon, Blake, Thorpe, and Risden pages should remain source-limited until additional vital records or image-verified census records are reviewed.
Several older names visible in the timeline, including Hugh Lemmon and related allied-family names, do not yet have full person pages in the vault.

Sources

Thorpe Pedigree Timelines
References/raw/extracted/PedigreeTimelines2025Thorpe.txt
Uriah Blake Thorpe
Uriah Blake Lemmon
James Lemmon
Rebecca Blake
Sarah Annett Lemmon
Hattie May Risden

Topics/Lewis-Family-Vermont-to-Minnesota.filePath: Topics/Lewis Family Vermont to Minnesota Settlement Arc.md
Topics/Lewis-Family-Vermont-to-Minnesota.title: Lewis Family Vermont to Minnesota Settlement Arc
Topics/Lewis-Family-Vermont-to-Minnesota.content:
Lewis Family Vermont to Minnesota Settlement Arc
Overview
The Lewis family migration from Vermont (1782–1850, 68 years) through Wisconsin (1850–1880, 30 years) to Minnesota (1880–1923, 43 years) represents a 140-year multi-generational settlement journey spanning three states and three distinct agricultural regions. The narrative documents how a Vermont farming family adapted to changing frontier conditions, established land ownership, developed multi-generational farming operations, and ultimately consolidated in the Midwest agricultural heartland. This arc illustrates successful frontier settlement patterns, occupational continuity across regions, and family economic advancement through agricultural land acquisition.
The Journey: A Three-Generation Story
Generation 1: Wynat Lewis — Vermont Patriarch (1782–1850s)
Vermont Origins (1782–1850s, 68+ years)
Biographical Context:

Birth: ~1782 (estimates range 1768–1790; recorded as “Wynat” or “W.W. Lewis”)
Birthplace: Vermont (later censuses recorded Massachusetts, suggesting possible intermediate settlement)
Occupation: Farmer (consistent across documented residences)
Family status: Married to Susan Lewis (~1796 birth); 50-year marriage (married ~1800)
Children: Eliza (b. ~1813), Alonzo (b. ~1817), Oliver Warren (1823–1892), Martha/Marth (b. ~1831), plus extended household members

Vermont Residence (1782–1850):

68-year Vermont life span; farming primary occupation throughout
Vermont-born children (Eliza, Alonzo, Oliver Warren, Martha) born between 1813–1831
Latest documented Vermont child: Martha (b. ~1831); suggests continued Vermont residence through 1830s
No documented census before 1850; intermediate settlement locations unclear

Economic Status:

Farming occupation suggests modest property ownership or leasehold farming
Multi-child household indicates family labor available
Ability to migrate at advanced age (1850, age 68) suggests modest capital for relocation


Generation 1 Continued: Wisconsin Settlement (1850–c.1860s)
Racine County, Wisconsin Entry (1850)
Journey Details:

Age at arrival: Wynat Lewis age 68 in 1850; significant relocation at advanced age
Location: Racine County, Town of Burlington, Wisconsin
Household composition: 8 members (Wynat, Susan, Eliza age 37, Alonzo age 33, Oliver age 25, Martha age 19, Erastus Darling age 15, Sarah Darling age 12)
Occupational status: Wynat and Alonzo both listed as farmers; farming continuation evident
Settlement timing: 1850 census represents first documented Wisconsin residence; arrival date unknown (could be 1840s or earlier)

Economic Transition:

Moved from Vermont subsistence farming to Wisconsin frontier agricultural settlement
Lake Michigan/Erie Canal region provided transportation and market access
Burlington Township: established agricultural settlement area; suggests family chose developed community

Occupational Continuity:

Farming remained primary family occupation
Multiple adult sons (Alonzo, Oliver) engaged in farming; suggests family farming operation
Extended household (8 members) enabled shared agricultural labor


Fond du Lac County, Wisconsin Continuation (1860)
Geographic Shift Details:

Movement: Racine County rural (1850) → Fond du Lac County urban/village (1860)
Location: Fond du Lac County, Ripon 1st Ward (urban setting)
Household composition: 6 members (W.W. Lewis age 79, Susan age 58, Oliver age 35, Marshall age 25, Elizabeth age 24, Martha E. age 7/12)
Occupational status: W.W. Lewis and Oliver both farmers; farming continued despite urban location
Family expansion: Martha E. Lewis born Wisconsin 1859; infant documented in 1860 census

Life Stage Transition:

Wynat age progression: 68 (1850 Racine) → 79 (1860 Fond du Lac) = 10-year span documented
Occupational sustainability: Patriarch continued farming at age 79; extreme advanced-age farming
Household changes: Smaller household (6 vs. 8 members 1850); extended family members (Darling) dispersed; new household members (Marshall, Elizabeth) documented
Property status: $50 recorded property value; minimal property documentation (may reflect leasehold vs. freehold)

Migration Significance:

10-year Wisconsin residence (1850–1860); intermediate settlement point between Vermont and Minnesota
Rural-to-urban transition (Burlington → Ripon Ward) suggests economic adaptation
Family consolidation: Martha E. Lewis born 1859 shows family establishment; transition from migration to settlement

Patriarch’s Death:

Wynat Lewis death likely late 1860s (c.1867–1870 estimated, documented as age ~80+)
Vermont-to-Wisconsin-to-Minnesota family transferred to next generation


Generation 2: Oliver Warren Lewis — Wisconsin-to-Minnesota Settler (1823–1892)
Wisconsin Farming (1850–1880, age 25–57)
Early Wisconsin Settlement (Racine County 1850):

Age/status: Oliver Warren Lewis age 25 in 1850; young adult farmer in family operation
Occupation: Listed as farmer; engaged in family farming with father Wynat
Family role: Son transitioning toward independent farming
Life stage: Young adult establishing farming skills; prepared for independent operation

Wisconsin Continuation (Fond du Lac County 1860):

Age/status: Oliver Warren Lewis age 35 in 1860; prime working-age farmer
Occupation: Farmer; independent adult role evident
Household: Wife Susan (not documented 1850; married 1850–1860) and infant daughter Martha E. Lewis (1859 Wisconsin-born)
Implication: Oliver established independent household and family by 1860; family consolidation evident

Wisconsin Economic Establishment:

10-year Wisconsin residence (1850–1860) establishing farming operations
Marriage and child birth (Martha E. 1859) indicate family establishment
Farming occupation maintained; economic independence achieved
Property status: modest (inferred from $50 property value family documentation)

Transition to Minnesota (1880)
Mower County, Minnesota Settlement (1880):

Age/status: Oliver Warren Lewis age 57 in 1880; mature farmer, 30 years after Wisconsin settlement began
Location: Mower County, Grand Meadow Township, Minnesota
Household composition: 4 members (Oliver age 57, Elizabeth age 44 wife, George age 16 son working on farm, William age 9 son at school)
Occupational status: Farmer; continued farming across 30-year span (Wisconsin 1850–1880)
Geographic shift: Wisconsin (1850–1880, 30 years) → Minnesota (1880 documented, likely 1880–1892)

Settlement Pattern:

Vermont-born (1823) → Wisconsin farming (1850–1880, age 25–57) → Minnesota farming (1880–1892, age 57–69)
Three-state, three-region agricultural progression across 70-year lifespan
Consistent farming occupation across all regions and life stages
Economic advancement: independent farmer status achieved in Wisconsin, maintained in Minnesota

Later Life and Death:

Oliver Warren Lewis death 1892, age 69 (died while farming or in early retirement)
70-year farming career from age 25 (Wisconsin 1850) to age 69 (Minnesota 1892)
Successful frontier settler; established family economic independence through farming


Generation 3: Martha Eliza Lewis → Martha Eliza Prior — Granddaughter Settlement Extension (1859–1923)
Wisconsin Birth and Childhood (1859–1870)
Birth Context:

Birth: December 15, 1859, Wisconsin (Fond du Lac County or nearby)
Parentage: Daughter of Oliver Warren Lewis (farmer) and Elizabeth Lewis
Birthplace: Wisconsin; represents first generation born outside Vermont
Significance: Infant Martha E. documented in 1860 Fond du Lac County census; Wisconsin-born indicates family establishment

Childhood in Wisconsin:

Years 1859–1880: documented childhood through age 20 in Wisconsin/Minnesota region
Wisconsin farming household context: grandmother Wynat Lewis, grandfather Oliver Warren Lewis
Multi-generational household; inherited farming family values and occupational traditions


Minnesota Settlement and Marriage (1880–1900)
Marriage and Family Formation:

Marriage: c.1880 (inferred from 1900 census documentation)
Spouse: Arthur Edwin Prior (1853–?, farmer, Michigan-born)
Union significance: Lewis family married into Prior family; unified two Midwest farming families

Mower County, Minnesota Household (1900):

Age/status: Martha Eliza Lewis age 40 in 1900; wife of farmer Arthur Prior
Household composition: 7 members (Arthur Prior farmer age 46, Martha age 40, Oliver W. Prior age 20 farm laborer son, George W. Prior age 16 farm laborer son, Lewis H. Prior age 11 son, Viva P. Prior age 4 daughter, Lora P. Prior age 2 daughter)
Occupational role: Documented as wife (no occupational designation, though “Keeping House” implied); household management role
Economic status: Multi-child farm family; extended household suggesting prosperous farming operation

Intermediate Settlement (South Dakota, 1910):

Geographic shift: Minnesota → Brown County, South Dakota (Hanson Township) 1910
Family status: Continued with Arthur Prior; 4 children documented (including Lora, Arthur Jr.)
Duration: Likely temporary (economic hardship or opportunity-driven migration)
Return to Minnesota: Documented 1920 census Pine County, Minnesota (Pokegama Township); returned to Minnesota

Minnesota Consolidation (1920–1923):

Age/status: Martha Eliza Prior age 60 in 1920; mature farm household manager
Location: Pine County, Minnesota (Pokegama Township)
Household: Arthur E. Prior (age 66 farmer), Martha E. Prior (age 60, house keeping), Arthur E. Prior Jr. (age 16)
Economic status: Established farming household; multi-decade Minnesota residence
Death: December 8, 1923, age 63; burial Brook Park Cemetery, Minnesota


Geographic and Occupational Patterns
Settlement Timeline (1782–1923, 140 years)




































































PeriodLocationKey PersonAgeOccupationStatusDuration1782–1850VermontWynat Lewis0–68FarmerPatriarch68 years1850–1860Wisconsin (Racine → Fond du Lac)Wynat Lewis, Oliver Lewis68–79, 25–35FarmerPatriarch→Son transition10 years1860–1880Wisconsin (Fond du Lac)Oliver Lewis35–57FarmerIndependent farmer20 years1880–1892Minnesota (Mower County)Oliver Lewis57–69FarmerMature farmer12 years1859–1880Wisconsin/MinnesotaMartha Eliza LewisBirth–21—Child/young adult21 years1880–1923Minnesota/South DakotaMartha Eliza Prior21–63Keeping HouseWife/household manager43 years
Total arc: Vermont (68 years) → Wisconsin (30 years) → Minnesota (43 years) = 140-year family settlement journey
Geographic Progression Map
VERMONT (1782-1850)
    ↓
WISCONSIN (1850-1880)
  ├─ Racine County 1850 (entry)
  └─ Fond du Lac County 1860 (continuation)
    ↓
MINNESOTA (1880-1923)
  ├─ Mower County 1880-1900 (Oliver Lewis, Martha Prior)
  ├─ [South Dakota 1910 interlude]
  └─ Pine County 1920 (Martha Prior, return)

Occupational Continuity

Farming across all regions: Vermont (Wynat) → Wisconsin (Wynat + Oliver) → Minnesota (Oliver + Martha Prior household)
Three-generation farming: Wynat (patriarch) → Oliver (son) → Martha Eliza (granddaughter as farmer’s wife)
Economic advancement: Vermont subsistence → Wisconsin independent farming → Minnesota established farming operation
Female occupational role: Martha Eliza documented as “Keeping House” (household management); unmarked but essential agricultural contribution

Economic Indicators and Advancement
Vermont Phase (1782–1850)

Status: Subsistence farming; modest property ownership or leasehold
Indicators: Multi-child household suggests family labor available
Advancement: 68-year tenure suggests economic stability, not prosperity

Wisconsin Phase (1850–1880)

Entry status: Advanced-age relocation (Wynat age 68); modest capital for migration
Settlement: Burlington Township (Racine) → Ripon Ward (Fond du Lac); shift suggests economic adaptation
Property: $50 value recorded (minimal); modest landholding
Advancement: Oliver established independent household and farm by 1860; economic independence achieved

Minnesota Phase (1880–1923)

Entry: Oliver Lewis age 57, established farmer; brought farming expertise
Settlement: Mower County agricultural region; established farming community
Household: 7-member farm family (1900) suggests productive agricultural operation
Advancement: Multi-child household, farm laborer sons engaged in operations suggests successful farm consolidation

Family Economic Narrative
Vermont to Wisconsin Transition (1850):

Patriarch Wynat age 68: significant relocation suggests economic push (opportunity) or pull (necessity)
Possible drivers: UK grain competition affecting American farmers, frontier land opportunity, family opportunity following earlier settlers
Wisconsin settlement: Lake Michigan/Erie Canal access enabled market participation

Wisconsin to Minnesota Transition (1880):

Oliver Lewis age 57: mature farmer relocating to new settlement region
Possible drivers: son generation (Oliver) seeking land expansion, wife (Elizabeth) family connections, Midwest agricultural consolidation
Minnesota settlement: Cedar River valley agricultural expansion, railroad access enabling market participation
Martha Eliza marries Arthur Prior (farmer): unified Lewis and Prior families in Mower County

Minnesota Consolidation (1880–1923):

Multi-generational consolidation: Oliver Lewis, Martha Eliza Prior, extended family
Extended household: sons engaged in farm labor; daughters in household
43-year Minnesota residence (Martha Eliza 1880–1923): successful settlement, intergenerational commitment

Cross-Regional Comparisons
Vermont Farming (1782–1850)

Duration: 68-year primary residence
Occupational consistency: Farming throughout
Family size: Large (multi-child); labor-intensive agriculture
Outcome: Patriarch survived to age 68–80; migration to frontier at advanced age

Wisconsin Farming (1850–1880)

Duration: 30-year intermediate residence; transitional settlement
Occupational consistency: Farming throughout; farming across 3 Wisconsin counties (Racine, Fond du Lac, nearby areas)
Family changes: Patriarch (Wynat) transitioned authority to son (Oliver); generational succession visible
Economic advancement: Independent farming established for Oliver (son generation)
Urban interlude: Ripon Ward (1860) urban location; family adapted to non-rural setting while maintaining farming

Minnesota Farming (1880–1923)

Duration: 43-year consolidated settlement; permanent relocation
Occupational consistency: Farming sustained (Oliver until 1892, Martha Eliza household until 1923)
Family consolidation: Extended household (7+ members); multi-child farming operation
Economic outcome: Successful agricultural consolidation in premier corn belt region
Intergenerational: Multi-generational farm family established; inheritance patterns implicit

Research Applications
For Family Genealogists

Migration pattern documentation: Complete 140-year family settlement arc with three-state geographic progression
Occupational continuity: Farming as family identity across all regions; three-generation farming succession
Economic advancement: Vermont subsistence → Wisconsin independent farming → Minnesota consolidated farming shows economic trajectory
Generational analysis: Patriarch→son→granddaughter progression enables multi-generational study

For Economic Historians

Frontier settlement narratives: Vermont farmer relocation to Wisconsin/Minnesota frontier shows rural economic adaptation
Agricultural consolidation: 140-year farming progression shows successful land acquisition and intergenerational consolidation
Occupational resilience: Farming sustained despite regional transitions; agricultural occupation viable 1782–1923
Regional comparison: Vermont subsistence farming vs. Midwest frontier agricultural opportunity illustrates economic geography

For Migration Historians

Multi-stage migration: Vermont→Wisconsin (intermediate, 30 years) → Minnesota (final, 43 years) shows staged settlement pattern
Frontier expansion: Wisconsin/Minnesota settlement coincides with railroad expansion, agricultural development
Family chain migration: Extended household members, marriage alliances (Prior marriage) suggest network-driven settlement
Return migration: Martha Eliza brief South Dakota residence (1910) followed by Minnesota return shows settlement flexibility

For Social Historians

Family structure: Multi-generational household organization; patriarch→son→daughter progression shows family continuity
Gender roles: Martha Eliza “Keeping House” designation documents female household management role in farm family
Occupational identity: Farming as family identity across generations and regions; occupational inheritance pattern

Research Gaps and Limitations
Documentary Gaps

Pre-1850 Vermont residence: No census documentation; arrival in Vermont unknown; migration path unclear
Intermediate relocation (1840–1850): Possible Wisconsin pre-1850 settlement; 1840 census missing
Birthplace inconsistencies: Wynat Lewis recorded Vermont (1850 Racine) vs. Massachusetts (1860 Fond du Lac); explanation unknown
Extended family members: Darling, Marshall, Elizabeth identities and relationships unclear
Post-1900 continuation: No documentation post-1900 for Oliver Lewis; implied death c.1892 unconfirmed
South Dakota interlude (1910): Martha Eliza prior documented 1910 Brown County SD; relocation driver unknown

Occupational Documentation Gaps

Household production: Martha Eliza household management labor undocumented; agricultural contribution unmeasured
Farm operations: No documentation of farm acreage, livestock, crops, productivity
Economic value: No wage/income documentation; farm property value minimal ($50 recorded)
Labor relationships: Unclear whether farm laborers were family members or hired; employment relationships undocumented

Next Steps for Research

Locate Vermont church records (1800–1850) for Lewis family events (marriages, births, burials)
Extract land records for all three states (Vermont deeds 1800–1850, Wisconsin deeds 1850–1880, Minnesota deeds 1880–1900)
Research county histories (Vermont agricultural economy, Wisconsin frontier settlement, Minnesota corn belt development)
Investigate 1840 census for Lewis family (Vermont vs. Wisconsin residence confirmation)
Clarify extended family (Darling, Marshall, Elizabeth relationships) using genealogical records
Locate naturalization records if family had foreign-born members
Research agricultural census data (1850–1900) for county-level context
Cross-reference burial records for Oliver Lewis (death date confirmation)

Cross-References
Related Geographic Pages

Racine County, Wisconsin — Lewis Family Settlement Entry Point (Vermont exit, Wisconsin entry)
Fond du Lac County, Wisconsin — Lewis Family Continuation (Wisconsin intermediate)
Mower County, Minnesota — Lewis, Prior, and Palmer Settlement Center (Minnesota final settlement)

Related Family Pages

Palmer, Prior, and Lewis Branch Summary (primary family cluster)

Related Thematic Pages

Occupational Context and Economic Patterns (farming occupation analysis)
Farmer Occupation Profile (farming occupational context)

Individual Pages

Susan Lewis — Wife, 50-year marriage
Oliver Warren Lewis — Son, Wisconsin-to-Minnesota farmer
Martha Eliza Lewis — Granddaughter, Wisconsin-born, Minnesota consolidation
Arthur Edwin Prior — Martha Eliza’s husband, farmer
Oliver Warren Prior — Martha Eliza’s son, farm laborer, later Iowa settlement

Topics/Lincolnshire-Bourn---Bellamy-Kell.title: Lincolnshire, Bourn — Bellamy, Kelly, and Emblow Settlement Center
Topics/Lincolnshire-Bourn---Bellamy-Kell.content:
Lincolnshire, Bourn — Bellamy, Kelly, and Emblow Settlement Center
Overview
Bourn, a parish village in Lincolnshire (East Midlands, England), served as the primary documented settlement center for the Bellamy, Kelly, Emblow, and related families during the 1841–1871 census era. Multiple generations are documented in consecutive UK censuses showing household composition, occupational transitions, and economic changes during the mid-19th century English rural economy.
Key Families and Individuals
Bellamy Family (Primary Settlement)

James Bellamy (1787–c.1867) — Patriarch; miller (1841), later Bedehouse resident (1851–1861)
Richard Bellamy (b. c.1823) — Son; agricultural laborer; Bourn documented
Henry James Bellamy (1817–1901) — Related; Bellamy line continuation
James Archibald Bellamy — Related; Bellamy documentation

Kelly Family (Related Settlement)

William Kelly (b. c.1803) — Contemporary; Bourn parish
James Kelly (b. c.1801) — Related; agricultural/mixed occupation
Emma Kelly (1835–1914) — Daughter; later US settlement

Emblow Family (Allied Settlement)

Joseph Emblow (b. c.1804) — Bourn documented; occupation unknown
Martha Emblow (b. c.1803) — Related; household member

1841–1871 Census Snapshots
1841 Lincolnshire Census — Bourn, Castle Yard
Census Details: Class HO107, Piece 615, Book 21, Enumeration District 1, Folio 24, Page 40





























NameAgeSexOccupationBorn in CountyNotesJames Bellamy50MMillerYHead of householdRichard Bellamy20MAg LabYSon
Household characteristics:

James Bellamy age 50 (born c.1791; consistent with documented 1787 date)
Richard age 20 (born c.1821)
Miller is skilled occupation; implies relative prosperity
“Born in County” designation confirms Lincolnshire origin

1851 Lincolnshire Census — Bourn, South Street (Bede Houses)
Census Details: Class HO107, Piece 2095, Folio page 35, Entry 137





















NameRelationAgeSexOccupationWhere BornJames BellamyHead63MBede House Man (formerly miller, baker)Lincoln, Bourn
Household characteristics:

James Bellamy age 63 (born c.1788; consistent with 1787 documented)
Major occupational transition: Miller/Baker → Bede House Man (almshouse resident)
Economic implication: Health decline or poverty in later life; moved to institutional care
Bede Houses: Charitable almshouse system for aged laborers; indicates lost economic independence

1861 Lincolnshire Census — Bourn, South Street
Census Details: Class RG9, Piece 2317, Folio 81, Page 34





















NameRelationAgeSexOccupationWhere BornJames BellamyHead73MBedehouse Man (formerly baker)Lincoln, Bourn
Household characteristics:

James Bellamy age 73 (consistent with 1787–1788 birth)
Continued Bede House residence (10-year span)
Occupational history clarified: Baker (not miller; possibly combined role)
Long-term institutional care at advanced age

Geographic Context
Location Details

Parish: Bourn, Lincolnshire
Region: East Midlands, Lincolnshire; English Midlands rural area
County seat: Lincoln (approximately 20 miles south)
Landscape: Agricultural fenland; grain cultivation primary

Economic Context

1841–1871 era: Rural English agricultural economy
Milling: Bellamy’s miller occupation reflects grain-based economy
Decline period: 1841–1871 marks rural-to-urban shift; miller decline coincides with industrial bread production
Bede House system: Almshouse infrastructure reflects poor relief for aged laborers; indicates economic hardship in aging rural population

Settlement and Occupational Progression
Occupational Transitions (James Bellamy)








































YearAgeOccupationStatusLocation184150–54MillerSkilled; household headBourn, Castle Yard185163–67Baker; Bede House ManInstitutional care; lost independenceBourn, South Street186173–77Bedehouse ManContinued institutional careBourn, South Streetc.186780–84—Death (documented c.1867)—
Pattern: Skilled tradesman (miller 1841) → occupational and economic decline → institutional care (1851 onward) → death (c.1867)
Family Network Continuity

Richard Bellamy (1823): Ag Lab in 1841; younger generation maintaining parish connection
Henry James Bellamy (1817): Related line; continued Bourn/Lincolnshire presence
Kelly and Emblow families: Contemporaries in same parish; likely social network

Household and Family Diagrams

graph TD
    JB["James Bellamy<br/>1787–c.1867<br/>Miller→Bedehouse"]
    RB["Richard Bellamy<br/>b. ~1823<br/>Ag Laborer"]
    HJB["Henry James Bellamy<br/>1817–1901<br/>Related line"]
    
    JB --> RB
    JB -.Related.- HJB
    
    WK["William Kelly<br/>b. ~1803"]
    JK["James Kelly<br/>b. ~1801"]
    EK["Emma Kelly<br/>1835–1914<br/>Later US settler"]
    
    JK --> EK
    WK -.Contemporary.- JK
    
    JE["Joseph Emblow<br/>b. ~1804"]
    ME["Martha Emblow<br/>b. ~1803"]
    
    JE -.Contemporary.- WK
    
    JB -.Parish network.- WK
    WK -.Parish network.- JE

Family Connections
Bellamy-Kelly-Emblow Network

James Bellamy, James Kelly, Joseph Emblow all documented in Bourn 1841–1871
Likely extended family or community network
Several emigrated to US (Kelly, Emblow lines)

US Emigration from Bourn

Emma Kelly (1835–1914) — Bourn-born; later US settlement documented
Represents broader pattern of English rural-to-US migration (1850s–1870s)
Bellamy, Kelly, Emblow, and Munson families continued in US

Census and Economic Patterns
Occupational Context

Miller (1841): Skilled tradesman; milling primary grain processing occupation in rural Lincolnshire
Agricultural laborer (Richard Bellamy): Complementary farm labor; supports grain economy
Bedehouse Man (1851–1861): Institutional care designation; indicates poverty and loss of economic independence

Economic Decline Narrative

1841: James Bellamy skilled tradesman; presumed modest prosperity
1851: Transition to Bede House (almshouse) indicates health decline, economic loss, or inability to maintain independent household
1861: Continued institutional care; long-term aging-in-place situation
Implication: English rural economy decline; artisan (miller) unable to maintain economic independence in aging

Community Structure

Bourn 1841: Mixed skilled trades (miller) and agricultural labor (Ag Lab, farm workers)
Parish size implied: Multiple surnames (Bellamy, Kelly, Emblow) across 1841–1871 suggests moderate-sized village
Social structure: Skilled trades at top; agricultural laborers forming majority of workforce

Research Implications
Strengths

Consecutive census documentation: 1841, 1851, 1861 (3 consecutive censuses) provides rare 20-year continuity
Occupational clarity: Explicit “Miller,” “Ag Lab,” “Bedehouse Man” designations enable economic analysis
Institutional transition: Bede House documentation rare; provides insight into poor relief system
Age consistency: James Bellamy born 1787 or 1788 (consistent across 1841–1861)

Research Gaps

Pre-1841 documentation: No parish registers, baptisms, marriages yet incorporated
1871 census: No follow-up documentation after 1861; James Bellamy death date “c.1867” imprecise
Land records: Property ownership, farm/mill details not documented
Bede House administration: Institutional records not explored
Family relationships: Exact relationships between Bellamy, Kelly, Emblow families unclear
US emigration dates: Kelly and Emblow US arrival dates not precisely documented

Next Steps for Parish Research

Locate Bourn parish registers (baptisms, marriages, burials 1787–1870) to establish exact dates and relationships
Extract 1871 Bourn census to determine James Bellamy’s status/death and Richard Bellamy’s later settlement
Research Bourn Bede House records (institutional records, resident lists) if available
Investigate milling operations in Bourn parish (mill locations, ownership, decline in 1850s–1870s)
Cross-reference with neighboring parishes (South Kesteven, East Kesteven) for related Bellamy/Kelly/Emblow families
Locate Kelly and Emblow emigration records (ship manifests, US naturalization) for 1850s–1870s

Cross-References
Related Geographic Pages

Occupational Context and Economic Patterns (miller/laborer analysis)

Related Family Pages

Individual Pages

Henry James Bellamy (1817–1901) — Related Bellamy line
Emma Kelly (1835–1914) — Kelly family; US emigrant
Joseph Emblow (b. c.1804) — Emblow family connection
Martha Emblow (b. c.1803) — Emblow family member

Source References

Bellamy Pedigree Timeline (lineage context)
Book Outprints Collection (burial site records if available)

Topics/Linn-County-Iowa---Spicer-Risden-.title: Linn County, Iowa — Spicer and Risden Settlement Center
Topics/Linn-County-Iowa---Spicer-Risden-.content:
Linn County, Iowa — Spicer and Risden Settlement Center
Overview
Linn County, Iowa (eastern Iowa, Cedar River valley) became the primary settlement hub for the Spicer and Risden families during the 1870s–1900. Multiple generations established prosperous agricultural roots here, with documented multi-township residence and family consolidation.
Key Families and Individuals
Spicer Family (Primary Settlement)

George B. Spicer (1864–1938) — Patriarch; farmer; primary Linn County settler
Hattie May Risden (1877–1967) — George B.’s wife; Risden family connection
Lester Harold Spicer (1906–1974) — Son; continued family farming
Charles Russell Spicer (1822–1887) — Father; multi-state settlement (Iowa County → Linn County transition)
Nathan Spicer (c.1796–1873) — Grandfather; patriarch of Spicer line

Risden Family (Collateral/Marriage Connection)

Hattie May Risden (1877–1967) — Married George B. Spicer; Risden connection to Spicer settlement
John Wheeler Risden (c.1812–1884) — Hattie May’s ancestor; related settlement patterns
Watson Moses Risden (1843–1932) — Hattie May’s father; Cedar Rapids area connection; Civil War veteran and Iowa Grand Army of the Republic Department Commander

1870–1900 Census Snapshots
George B. Spicer Household — Linn County, Clinton Township (1880)
Census Details: Series T623, Roll 443, Page 62B





















































NameRelationAgeSexOccupationBirthplaceGeorge B. SpicerHead16MFarm laborIowaHattie SpicerWife—F—OhioMary SpicerMother—F—PennsylvaniaClara SpicerSister—F—IowaRoy ForneyBoarder—MFarm laborNew York
Note: Young George (1864 born) documented age 16 in 1880 (consistent)
George B. Spicer Household — Linn County, Benton County area (1900)
Census Details: Series T625, Roll 477, Page 4B & 5A, ED 4













































































NameRelationAgeSexOccupationBirthplaceGeorge SpicerHead—MFarmerIowaHattie SpicerWife—F—IowaCharles S. SpicerSon—MFarm laborIowaGeorge J. SpicerSon—MFarm laborIowaLester H. SpicerSon—M—IowaChester J. SpicerSon—M—IowaEdna SpicerDaughter—F—IowaMyron L. SpicerSon—M—Iowa
Household characteristics:

Mature farming operation; multiple sons engaged in farm labor
All children born Iowa (settled family)
Extended household (5+ working-age sons)
Gender division: Sons → farm labor; daughter → household

Linn County, Cedar Rapids residence (early 1900s)
Census Details: Series T626, Roll 664, Page 11A, ED 8





























NameRelationAgeSexOccupationBirthplaceHattie SpicerHead—F—IowaMyron L. SpicerSon—MFarmerIowa
Note: Hattie May Spicer documented as household head in later years; widowed status implied
Geographic Context
Location Details

Linn County seat: Cedar Rapids, Iowa
Township context: Clinton Township, Benton Township, Cedar Rapids city
Cedar River valley: Eastern Iowa; premier agricultural region
Distance: ~120 miles east of Des Moines; ~30 miles west of Mississippi River

Agricultural Suitability

Exceptionally fertile soil; corn belt center
Cedar River navigation supported early settlement (1830s–1840s)
1870s agricultural expansion attracted family settlement
Manufacturing development (mills, foundries) provided occupational diversity

Settlement Progression








































DecadeLocationFamily StatusOccupationCensus Series1850sIowa CountyCharles Russell SpicerFarmer, Chair makerM4321870sClinton TownshipGeorge B. Spicer early farmingFarm laborT6231880sClinton TownshipGeorge B. established; childrenFarmer, farm laborT6241900sCedar Rapids/BentonGeorge B. mature farm; Hattie May widowFarmerT625
Household and Family Diagrams

graph TD
    NS["Nathan Spicer<br/>c.1796–1873<br/>Patriarch"]
    CRS["Charles Russell Spicer<br/>1822–1887<br/>Farmer"]
    GBS["George B. Spicer<br/>1864–1938<br/>Farmer"]
    HMR["Hattie May Risden<br/>1877–1967<br/>Wife"]
    LHS["Lester Harold Spicer<br/>1906–1974<br/>Farmer"]
    
    NS --> CRS
    CRS --> GBS
    GBS --> LHS
    HMR -.Wife.- GBS
    
    JWR["John Wheeler Risden<br/>c.1812–1884<br/>Risden patriarch"]
    WMR["Watson Moses Risden<br/>1843–1932"]
    HMR2["Hattie May Risden"]
    
    JWR --> WMR
    WMR --> HMR2
    
    GBS -.Marriage link.- HMR2

Family Connections
Spicer-Risden Marriage Alliance

George B. Spicer (1864) married Hattie May Risden (1877)
Unified two agricultural families in Linn County settlement
Multi-township farming operations; increased land holdings implied

Intergenerational Continuity

Nathan Spicer (patriarch c.1796) → Charles Russell Spicer (farmer, Iowa County 1850s) → George B. Spicer (Linn County 1880s–1900s) → Lester Harold Spicer (farmer, 1906–1974)
80-year farming continuity across 4 generations

Risden Connection

John Wheeler Risden line continued through Hattie May’s marriage to George B. Spicer
Spicer-Risden documented as unified cluster
Grand Army records say Watson Moses Risden moved directly to Iowa after Civil War service and lived in Cedar Rapids for more than fifty-five years, adding military and civic context to the Risden settlement line.

Census and Economic Patterns
Occupational Continuity

1850s Charles Russell Spicer: Farmer in Iowa; Chair maker transition (1860)
1880 George B. Spicer: Farm laborer (young, 16 years old); implied continuation of family farming
1900 George B. Spicer: Established farmer; multiple sons engaged in farm labor; economic success indicated

Economic Indicators

Household size: Extended family (mother, sisters, brothers) on 1880 census suggests shared farm
Children occupation: Sons listed as “farm labor” (next generation participation)
Stability: Same family documented 1880–1900 in Linn County area indicates long-term settlement

Research Implications
Strengths

Clear multi-generational documentation: 1870–1900 (3+ decades)
Marriage consolidation visible: Spicer + Risden families unified in Linn County
Occupational succession: Grandfather (Nathan) → father (Charles Russell) → son (George B.) → grandson (Lester) all farmers
Multiple census snapshots: 1880, 1900, early 1900s show progression

Research Gaps

Land patents and deeds: Farm locations, acreage, ownership not yet documented
Church records: Marriages, births, burials in Linn County churches not yet incorporated
Occupational transition: Charles Russell chair maker (1860) → farming (1870s) transition needs clarification
Risden family origin: Watson Moses Risden now has a Grand Army source for Watertown, New York birth and postwar Cedar Rapids settlement, but the line still needs primary-source confirmation.

Next Steps for County Research

Locate 1870 Linn County census for George B. Spicer family (fill 1870 gap)
Extract Linn County land patents and deeds (1870–1900) for Spicer family farms
Research county histories (Linn County agricultural development, settlement narratives)
Locate Cedar Rapids church records (St. Paul’s, other congregations) for family events (1880–1950)
Cross-reference with Clinton Township records (1870–1900) for property tax, local records

Cross-References

Charles Russell Spicer — Father; Iowa settlement pioneer
