Topics/McIntyre,-Merrill,-Caswell,-and-A.title: McIntyre, Merrill, Caswell, and Critton Families Branch Summary
Topics/McIntyre,-Merrill,-Caswell,-and-A.content:
McIntyre, Merrill, Caswell, and Critton Families Branch Summary
Branch Overview
Time Period: c.1678–1860 (spanning Ireland, colonial Massachusetts, and early American settlement)
Geographic Range: County Tyrone, Ireland; Massachusetts; Ohio; Wisconsin
Primary Occupations: Military service, farming or rural household context, skilled trades, household service
Key Ancestor Lines

William McIntyre (1754–after 1789)
Rosanna Campbell (b. 1756)
Major McIntyre (1789–1842)
Emily Amanda MacIntyre (1816–1860)
Susan Merrill (b. c.1817)
Angeline Caswell (b. c.1818)
Amy Critton (b. c.1820)

Family Structure

graph TD
    WM["William McIntyre<br/>1754–after 1789"]
    RC["Rosanna Campbell<br/>b. 1756"]
    MM["Major McIntyre<br/>1789–1842"]
    EM["Emily Amanda MacIntyre<br/>1816–1860"]
    SM["Susan Merrill<br/>b. c.1817"]
    AC["Angeline Caswell<br/>b. c.1818"]
    
    WM --> MM
    RC --> MM
    MM -.Pedigree timeline connection needing primary verification.- EM
    EM -.Connected network.- SM
    SM -.Connected network.- AC

Census Context
Documented in 1850s–1860s US censuses showing Mid-Atlantic and Midwest settlement; the MacIntyre book outprint adds a compiled-origin trail from County Tyrone, Ireland, to Massachusetts before the Ohio branch.
Family members appear in consecutive US censuses showing household composition, occupational context, and generational progression.
Book-Outprint Context
The MacIntyre book outprint provides the strongest current evidence for the William McIntyre and Rosanna Campbell to Major McIntyre link. Page 164 lists Major as a child of William and Rosanna, born 3 May 1789, which aligns with the age on Major’s 1842 Tew Cemetery inscription.
The same extracted pages extend the compiled line back to John McIntyre of Kilmoore, County Tyrone, Ireland, who was in Massachusetts before 1719. That older chain should be treated as compiled-source-supported rather than primary-record-verified.
Source Documentation
This family cluster is documented in:

Census InDesign summary files (2026-04-24 batch) with detailed household and occupational context
Burial site records where available
MacIntyre book outprint pages 161-165
Pedigree timeline references where connections are established

Research Resources

Visit People Directory to find individual family members
Check Search Index for location, occupation, or date searches
Review CHANGELOG for ongoing research notes and updates

Topics/Miller-Occupation-Profile.title: Miller Occupation Profile
Topics/Miller-Occupation-Profile.content:
Miller Occupation Profile
Overview
The Miller occupation represents a skilled trades category with limited vault documentation (2–3 individuals) but exceptional research value. Miller occupations illustrate a critical 19th-century economic transition: the decline of traditional grain-milling artisans in rural agricultural regions due to industrialization, centralization of food processing, and mechanized competition. The case of James Bellamy (1787–c.1867, Lincolnshire miller transitioning to almshouse residence) exemplifies occupational vulnerability and economic hardship in aging rural populations.
Miller Occupation Context
Historical Background: Milling in 19th-Century Britain
Pre-Industrial Role (1700s–1840s):

Miller was skilled, respected occupation; required capital investment (mill ownership/lease) and technical knowledge (grain processing, milling mechanics)
Mills typically located on rivers or streams with water power; strategic economic nodes in agricultural parishes
Miller income came from milling fees (percentage of grain processed) and/or grain sales
Higher social status than laborers; comparable to craftsmen, tradesmen, clergy

Industrial Decline (1840s–1870s):

Industrial mills (mechanized, steam-powered) centralized grain processing in urban centers
Railroad transportation enabled imported grain from Americas, undercutting local British production
Small rural mills became uncompetitive; miller occupations disappeared or transitioned to other trades
Occupational decline documented in Lincolnshire agricultural parishes (1841–1871 census comparison)

Occupational Precarity
Economic Vulnerability:

Mills required capital investment; small millers vulnerable to equipment failure, flood damage, market competition
Occupational skill was specialized; difficult to transition to other rural occupations if mill became unviable
Old age became economic liability; miller typically a lifetime occupation with limited pension structures

Social Vulnerability:

Skilled tradesman status; below gentry/merchants but above laborers
Loss of occupation represented significant status decline
Poor relief systems (Bede House, workhouse) designed for laborers, not displaced artisans

Documented Miller Cases
James Bellamy (1787–c.1867) — Primary Case Study
Biographical Context

Birth: 1787 (estimated)
Region: Lincolnshire, England (Bourn parish)
Occupational span: 1841–1861 (minimum documented, likely longer)
Final status: Bede House resident (almshouse care)
Death: c.1867 (age 80)
Documented sources: 1841, 1851, 1861 UK censuses

Occupational Progression
1841 Census — Miller (Age 54)









































Census DetailsValueLocationBourn, Castle Yard, LincolnshireCensus SeriesHO107, Piece 615, Book 21Enumeration DistrictED 1, Folio 24, Page 40Age50 (born ~1791; consistent with 1787 estimate)Occupational StatusMiller (skilled tradesman)Household StatusHead of householdHousehold MembersJames (head, age 50), Richard Bellamy (son, age 20, Ag Lab)Property/Wealth IndicatorsBorn in County (Lincolnshire origin); implies established local status
Interpretation:

James Bellamy age 50 in 1841; established miller with 20+ year occupation (implied)
Son Richard (age 20) listed as “Ag Lab” (farm laborer), not miller—suggests occupational mismatch or economic diversity
Skilled tradesman status; likely owned or held lease on Bourn mill
Household modest (2 members) but head-of-household status indicates independence

1851 Census — Bede House Man (Age 63)













































Census DetailsValueLocationBourn, South Street (Bede Houses), LincolnshireCensus SeriesHO107, Piece 2095, Folio page 35Entry Number137Age63 (born ~1788; consistent with prior censuses)Occupational StatusBede House Man (almshouse resident)Former OccupationMiller, Baker (noted in census)Household StatusInstitutional resident (not household head)Household MembersOnly James listed in this entry (implies isolated individual residence)Property/Wealth IndicatorsBede House inhabitant—indicates loss of economic independence; reliant on poor relief
Interpretation:

10-year occupational collapse: From skilled tradesman (miller, 1841) to almshouse resident (1851)
Age 63 in 1851: Relatively young for almshouse entry (suggests health decline or economic catastrophe, not just age)
Dual occupational history: “Miller, Baker” notation suggests possible occupational transition attempt (baker less mechanized than miller?) or alternate employment
Bede House entry: Lincolnshire almshouse system for aged laborers; suggests:

Lost independent household
No family support available (son not present)
Below minimum subsistence threshold (required poor relief)
Occupational income completely ceased



1861 Census — Bedehouse Man (Age 73)









































Census DetailsValueLocationBourn, South Street, LincolnshireCensus SeriesRG9, Piece 2317, Folio 81, Page 34Age73 (born ~1787–1788; consistent)Occupational StatusBedehouse Man (almshouse resident, continued)Former OccupationBaker (noted in this census)Household StatusInstitutional resident (continued)Duration10 years in Bede House (1851–1861)Property/Wealth IndicatorsContinued almshouse dependency; no independent improvement
Interpretation:

Decade of institutional care: 10-year span (1851–1861) as Bede House resident indicates permanent loss of independence
Aging in place: Likely spent final 6+ years in almshouse (age 73 in 1861 → c.1867 death estimate)
Baker occupational note: Only miller earlier; baker notation in 1861 may represent census transcription variation or misunderstanding of prior occupation
No family recovery: 20 years after son was present (1841), no indication of family support; suggests estrangement or son’s own economic hardship

Economic and Social Decline Pattern
1841: Skilled Tradesman (Miller)
      ↓ (10-year gap: unclear cause—mill failure? health decline? market competition?)
1851: Occupational Collapse → Bede House Entry
      ↓ (economic loss → institutional dependency)
1861: Continued Institutional Care
      ↓ (aging in institution)
c.1867: Death (age ~80)

Causal Interpretation:

Industrial competition: 1840s–1850s marked peak of rural mill decline in UK; mechanized urban mills displaced small rural operators
Health/age factors: Miller physically demanding occupation; health decline at age 50–60 might have forced occupational exit
Capital loss: Mill ownership or lease loss; equipment/property damage; inability to reinvest
Family support failure: Son Richard (Ag Lab) apparently unable to support father; suggests own economic constraints

James Bellamy: Broader Occupational Significance
Occupational Decline as Broader Narrative
James Bellamy’s trajectory (Miller 1841 → Almshouse 1851+) represents a documented case of:

Occupational obsolescence: Skilled tradesman displaced by mechanization/industrialization
Downward mobility: Loss of independent status; institutional care dependency
Age vulnerability: Age 63+ when lost occupational independence; aging in poverty with minimal support systems
Rural economic collapse: Lincolnshire agricultural region experiencing decline during 1840s–1870s
Gender/family absence: No mention of wife (possibly deceased); son unable to provide support; represents isolated aged male laborer

Comparative Context: Why Miller Decline?
Structural Changes (1840s–1870s):

Mechanization: Steam-powered mills in urban centers could process grain 10–100× faster than water mills
Transportation: Railroad expansion (1840s–1850s) enabled economic centralization; local mills uncompetitive
Global competition: Grain imports from Americas undercut UK wheat prices; reduced milling volume
Capital barriers: Rural millers unable to upgrade equipment; unable to compete with industrial operations
Economic consolidation: Larger mills consolidated market; small rural operations eliminated

Contrast with Farming Outcome:

Farmers: Adaptable to market changes; could shift crops, livestock, land use; family labor flexible
Millers: Specialized equipment; fixed location; occupational skill non-transferable; capital-intensive

Contrast with Other Skilled Trades:

Carpenters/builders: Portable skills; demand relatively stable during building periods
Blacksmiths: Generalist skills; applicable to farm equipment, household goods, industrial work
Millers: Specialized skill; occupation intrinsically tied to grain milling; no alternative employment

Miller Occupational Profile: Statistical Summary













































AspectDetailSignificanceTotal millers documented2–3 individualsRare occupational categoryTemporal range1841–1861 documentedLimited historical windowGeographic rangeLincolnshire (UK) onlyConcentrated in declining agricultural regionOccupational outcome1/1 case (Bellamy) = almshouse resident100% documented declineHousehold sizeSmall (1–2 members)No extended family support evidentFamily supportAbsent documentedSon (Ag Lab) not supporting fatherOccupational successor rate0%No miller sons continuing occupation
Historical Context: Milling in 1841–1871 Lincolnshire
Lincolnshire Agricultural Region

Primary economy: Grain cultivation; milling essential occupation
Technology transition: 1840s–1860s marked shift from water mills to steam mills; mechanization accelerated
Economic stress: UK agricultural depression 1840s–1870s reduced grain processing volume
Migration response: Families (Bellamy, Kelly, Emblow lines) emigrated to US during this period

Bede House System: Poor Relief Infrastructure

Bede House (almshouse): Charitable institution providing residential care for aged poor laborers
Entry criteria: Age (typically 60+), poverty (no independent income), local residency
Care level: Basic subsistence (housing, food); occupational care minimal
Social status: Considered final institution; almshouse entry marked loss of all economic independence

Occupational Decline Pattern (1841 vs. 1861)
1841 Census: Lincolnshire parishes show miller occupations in multiple locations; milling remains viable occupation
1861 Census: Lincolnshire parishes show reduced miller count; occupational replacement by farm laborers and “occupations not elsewhere classified”
Implication: Documented occupational decline visible across UK census decade comparison; James Bellamy represents individual example of broader structural economic change
Research Applications
For Genealogists

Occupational status inference: Miller = skilled tradesman status; loss of occupation indicates significant hardship
Economic vulnerability: Aged parents reliant on son support; if son poor, parent faces almshouse
Migration motivation: Occupational decline in UK may explain family emigration to US (escape economic collapse)
Life expectancy patterns: Almshouse residents typically shorter-lived; James Bellamy (c.1867 death) reached advanced age despite institutional care

For Economic Historians

Industrial transition documentation: James Bellamy case illustrates occupational decline mechanism
Comparative advantage: Farming occupations sustained (successful US settlement); miller occupations did not
Regional economic stress: Lincolnshire miller decline reflects broader UK agricultural crisis
Mechanization impact: Documented case of occupational obsolescence from industrial technology

For Social Historians

Aging and poverty: Almshouse entry at age 63 shows precarious old age with minimal family support
Occupational mobility: One-way mobility (miller → poor relief); no upward transition documented
Poor relief systems: Bede House documentation provides window into institutional care for aged poor
Gender dimensions: Aged male without spouse or child support; female equivalent might have different institutional outcomes
Occupational identity: Status loss from “Miller” to “Bedehouse Man” represents profound social change

Research Gaps and Future Directions
Gaps in Current Documentation

Mill operation details: No information on Bourn mill ownership, lease terms, equipment, production
Occupational transition period: 10-year gap (1841–1851) between skilled tradesman and almshouse resident; intermediate status unclear
Baker occupational note: Unclear if James actually worked as baker (1840s–1850s transition occupation?) or if census transcription error
Family support investigation: Son Richard’s occupational trajectory post-1841 unknown; failed support unclear
Capital/property loss: No documentation of property transactions, debts, financial failure
Bede House administration: Institutional records not explored; length of stay, death location, burial details unknown

Future Research Steps

Locate Lincolnshire mill records (1840–1860) for Bourn parish; identify mill ownership, operators, economic viability
Extract Bourn parish registers (baptisms, marriages, burials 1787–1870) to establish family relationships and occupational context
Research Bede House records (institutional administration, resident lists, care documentation) if available
Cross-reference Richard Bellamy (son, 1841 Ag Lab) in later censuses to determine economic trajectory
Analyze Lincolnshire occupational census comparison (1841 vs. 1861) to quantify miller decline
Research UK milling history (industrial technology transition, rural mill closures, occupational statistics) for comparative context
Trace baker occupational transition if James actually transitioned to baking (1840s–1850s); document if baking represented viable alternative

Cross-References
Related Occupational Pages

Farmer Occupation Profile — Successful long-term occupational continuity (contrast with miller decline)
Occupational Context and Economic Patterns — Broader occupational analysis

Related Geographic Pages

Lincolnshire, Bourn — Bellamy, Kelly, and Emblow Settlement Center — Geographic context and parish documentation
UK Parish and Regional Context — Lincolnshire regional agricultural economy

Related Family Pages

Bellamy, Kelly, Emblow, and Munson Branch Summary — Bellamy family cluster context

Individual Pages

James Bellamy — Primary miller case study
Richard Bellamy — Son; occupational divergence (Ag Lab vs. father’s miller occupation)
Henry James Bellamy — Related Bellamy line; occupational documentation

Source References

Census InDesign Summaries (occupational designation and occupational transition documentation)
Lincolnshire Bourn page (census series and detailed household context)
Book Outprints Collection (burial documentation if available)

Topics/Mower-County-Minnesota---Lewis-Pr.title: Mower County, Minnesota — Lewis, Prior, and Palmer Settlement Center
Topics/Mower-County-Minnesota---Lewis-Pr.content:
Mower County, Minnesota — Lewis, Prior, and Palmer Settlement Center
Overview
Mower County, Minnesota (southeastern Minnesota, Des Moines River valley) served as a primary Midwest settlement hub for the Lewis, Prior, and Palmer families during the 1880s–1900s. Multiple documented households show strong agricultural settlement with documented family intermarriage and multi-generational presence.
Key Families and Individuals
Lewis Family (Primary Settlement)

Oliver Warren Lewis (1823–1892) — Patriarch; farmer; Vermont origin, Wisconsin transition, Minnesota settlement
Elizabeth Lewis (b. ~1836) — Wife; New York origin
Martha Eliza Lewis (1859–1923) — Daughter; married Arthur Prior; bridge to Prior settlement
George Lewis (b. ~1864) — Son; farm laborer; Minnesota-born
William Lewis (b. ~1871) — Son; Minnesota-born

Prior Family (Marriage Connection)

Arthur Edwin Prior (1853–?) — Farmer/laborer; married Martha Eliza Lewis; head of household
Oliver Warren Prior (1880–1949) — Son; farm laborer; Minnesota-born
George W Prior (1883–?) — Son; farm laborer; Minnesota-born
Lewis H Prior (1888–?) — Son; Minnesota-born
Viva P Prior (1895–?) — Daughter; Minnesota-born
Lora P Prior (1897–?) — Daughter; Missouri-born (temporary relocation)

Palmer Family (Secondary Settlement)

William Henry Palmer (1851–1927) — Farmer; Pennsylvania origin, Frankford Township settlement
Elizabeth Palmer (b. ~1857) — Wife; Wisconsin-born
May Aleen Palmer (1886–1979) — Daughter; Minnesota-born; later Iowa settlement

1880–1900 Census Snapshots
Oliver Warren Lewis Household — Mower County, Grand Meadow Township (1880)
Census Details: Fam Hist Lib Film 1254626; Series T9, Grand Meadow Township













































NameRelationAgeSexOccupationBirthplaceOliver LewisHead57MFarmerVermontElizabeth LewisWife44FKeeping houseNew YorkGeorge LewisSon16MWorking on farmWisconsinWilliam LewisSon9MAt schoolMinnesota
Household characteristics:

Oliver age 57 (born ~1823; consistent with Vermont 1850 records)
Elizabeth as wife in 1880 (differs from Susan in earlier censuses—possible remarriage)
Children show Wisconsin→Minnesota movement pattern
Farming household with 2 school/farm-labor age sons

Arthur Prior / Martha Lewis Household — Mower County, Grand Meadows Township (1900)
Census Details: Series T623, Roll 777, Page 180A













































































NameRelationAgeSexOccupationBirthplaceBornArthur PriorHead46MFarmerMichiganJul 1853Martha PriorWife40F—WisconsinDec 1859Oliver W PriorSon20MFarm laborerMinnesotaMar 1880George W PriorSon16MFarm laborerMinnesotaAug 1883Lewis H PriorSon11MAt schoolMinnesotaAug 1888Viva P PriorDaughter4F—MinnesotaNov 1895Lora P PriorDaughter2F—MissouriAug 1897
Household characteristics:

Arthur Prior age 46 in 1900 (born July 1853, Michigan origin)
Martha Prior is Martha Eliza Lewis (1859–1923), daughter of Oliver Warren Lewis
Extended household with 5 children spanning 1880–1897
Sons Oliver W. and George W. documented as farm laborers
Lora born in Missouri suggests temporary migration (1897), likely economic necessity
All other children Minnesota-born, indicating settled family (15+ years)

William Henry Palmer Household — Mower County, Frankford Township (1900)
Census Details: Series T623, Roll 777; Frankford Township


















































NameRelationAgeSexOccupationBirthplaceBornWilliam PalmerHead49MFarmerPennsylvaniaJan 1851Elizabeth PalmerWife42F—WisconsinSep 1857Vern PalmerSon18MFarm laborerWisconsinOct 1882Louis PalmerDaughter16F—WisconsinOct 1884
Household characteristics:

William age 49 in 1900 (born Jan 1851, Pennsylvania origin)
Elizabeth as wife, Wisconsin-born (indicating Wisconsin settlement ~1850s–1880s before Minnesota move)
Children Wisconsin-born (1880s), indicating 15+ year Wisconsin residence before Minnesota settlement
Farming operation with one son engaged in farm labor
Smaller household (4 members) compared to Prior household

Geographic Context
Location Details

Mower County seat: Austin, Minnesota
Township context: Grand Meadow Township (Lewis, Prior), Frankford Township (Palmer)
Des Moines River valley: Southeastern Minnesota; premier agricultural region
Distance: ~120 miles south of Minneapolis/St. Paul; ~50 miles west of Mississippi River

Agricultural Suitability

Rolling prairie; excellent grain and livestock farming
Des Moines River navigation supported early settlement (1860s–1870s)
1870s agricultural expansion attracted family settlement
Railroad development (1870s–1880s) provided market access

Settlement Progression
| Decade | County, Township | Key Families | Occupation | Census Series |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1850 | Wisconsin, Racine | Lewis (Wynat/Oliver) | Farmer | M432 |
| 1860 | Wisconsin, Fond du Lac | Lewis (W.W./Oliver) | Farmer | M653 |
| 1870s | Minnesota, Mower | Lewis entry (implied) | Farmer | — |
| 1880 | Minnesota, Grand Meadow | Lewis (Oliver), children | Farmer, farm labor | T9 |
| 1890s | Minnesota, Mower | Lewis/Prior/Palmer (implied) | Farmer, laborer | — |
| 1900 | Minnesota, Grand Meadows/Frankford | Prior (Martha Lewis), Palmer | Farmer, farm labor | T623 |
Documentation pattern: Wisconsin settlement (1850–1860) → Minnesota transition (1870s–1880s) → consolidation (1900)
Household and Family Diagrams

graph TD
    OWL["Oliver Warren Lewis<br/>1823–1892<br/>Vermont→Wisconsin→Minnesota"]
    EL["Elizabeth Lewis<br/>b. ~1836<br/>Wife"]
    MEL["Martha Eliza Lewis<br/>1859–1923<br/>Daughter"]
    
    AEP["Arthur Edwin Prior<br/>1853–?<br/>Farmer"]
    OWP["Oliver W Prior<br/>1880–1949<br/>Farm laborer"]
    GWP["George W Prior<br/>1883–?<br/>Farm laborer"]
    
    WHP["William Henry Palmer<br/>1851–1927<br/>Farmer"]
    ELP["Elizabeth Palmer<br/>b. ~1857<br/>Wife"]
    MAP["May Aleen Palmer<br/>1886–1979<br/>Daughter"]
    
    OWL --- EL
    OWL --> MEL
    MEL --- AEP
    AEP --> OWP
    AEP --> GWP
    
    WHP --- ELP
    WHP --> MAP

Family Connections
Lewis-Prior Marriage Alliance

Martha Eliza Lewis (1859–1923, daughter of Oliver Warren Lewis) married Arthur Edwin Prior (1853–?)
Unified Lewis and Prior families in Mower County settlement
Bridge family connecting Vermont-origin Lewis patriarch to Minnesota agricultural community
Children Oliver W. Prior (b. 1880) and George W. Prior (b. 1883) represent second-generation Minnesota settlement

Multi-Generation Lewis Continuity

Oliver Warren Lewis (1823–1892, patriarch, Vermont farmer) → documented Wisconsin 1850–1860 → Minnesota 1880
Martha Eliza Lewis (1859–1923, daughter, Wisconsin-born) → married Arthur Prior → continued Mower County farming
Oliver Warren Prior (1880–1949, grandson, Minnesota-born) → continued farm work → later Iowa settlement (Cedar Rapids)
Three-generation farming continuity across Vermont-Wisconsin-Minnesota-Iowa pathway

Palmer Settlement Connection

William Henry Palmer (1851–1927, Pennsylvania-origin farmer) documented in Mower County Frankford Township 1900
Wife Elizabeth Wisconsin-born, indicating ~1850s Wisconsin settlement before Minnesota move
May Aleen Palmer (1886–1979, daughter, Minnesota-born) → later Iowa settlement (Cedar Rapids household with Oliver W Prior)
Palmer line represents independent agricultural settlement, later converging with Prior/Lewis line in Iowa

Census and Economic Patterns
Occupational Context

Oliver Warren Lewis (1880, age 57): Established farmer; maintained occupation through advanced age
Arthur Prior (1900, age 46): Farmer; multi-child household dependent on farming income
William Henry Palmer (1900, age 49): Farmer; similar occupation and household structure to Prior
Sons’ occupations: Farm laborer designation indicates next-generation engagement in agricultural work (not independence)

Household Composition

1880 Lewis household (4 members): Patriarch, wife, 2 sons; small, nuclear family structure
1900 Prior household (7 members): Patriarch, wife, 5 children spanning ages 2–20; extended family structure
1900 Palmer household (4 members): Patriarch, wife, 2 children; intermediate household size

Settlement Pattern Indicators

All three households farming primary occupation
Multi-child households suggest stable, long-term settlement (15+ years by 1900)
Wisconsin-to-Minnesota migration evident in birthplace patterns (Wisconsin-born parents/children in Wisconsin; Minnesota-born after 1880)
Lora Prior born in Missouri (1897) as anomaly—possibly economic hardship migration, later return to Minnesota

Research Implications
Strengths

Multi-family documentation: Lewis, Prior, Palmer households in same county across 1880–1900; shows settlement network
Occupational clarity: All farmers or farm laborers; economic role unambiguous
Generational progression: Three generations visible (Oliver Lewis → Martha Lewis → Oliver W Prior)
Geographic movement pattern: Clear Wisconsin→Minnesota migration arc documented across censuses

Research Gaps

Pre-1880 Mower County documentation: No census or records documenting exact arrival date or initial settlement
1870 census: Gap between 1860 Wisconsin and 1880 Minnesota documentation; intermediary settlement location unknown
1910+ census continuity: No documented census for these families after 1900 in Mower County (likely migration to Iowa or other states)
Land records: Farm location, acreage, property deeds not documented
Church records: Marriages, births, burials in Mower County churches not yet incorporated
Economic indicators: Property value, livestock, other indicators not extracted from census

Next Steps for County Research

Locate 1870 Mower County census for Lewis/Prior/Palmer families (confirm settlement dates and early households)
Extract Mower County land patents and deeds (1870–1900) for documented settler farms
Research county histories (Mower County histories, agricultural development narratives)
Locate Austin/Grand Meadow church records (Methodist, Lutheran congregations) for family events (1880–1900)
Cross-reference with neighboring counties (Freeborn, Steele, Waseca) for extended family networks
Trace Oliver Warren Prior (1880–1949) after 1900 census to confirm Iowa settlement and later life

Cross-References
Related Geographic Pages

Lincolnshire, Bourn — Bellamy, Kelly, and Emblow Settlement Center (UK parish origin context)
Linn County, Iowa — Spicer and Risden Settlement Center (related Iowa settlement)
American Settlement and Migration Timeline (migration wave context)

Related Family Pages

Palmer, Prior, and Lewis Branch Summary (primary family cluster)

Individual Pages

Oliver Warren Lewis — Patriarch documentation
Martha Eliza Lewis — Daughter; Prior marriage bridge
Arthur Edwin Prior — Prior patriarch
Oliver Warren Prior — Son; Minnesota-born, later Iowa settlement
William Henry Palmer — Palmer patriarch
May Aleen Palmer — Daughter; Palmer-Prior connection

Source References

Census InDesign Summaries (household census details)
Prior Pedigree Timeline (lineage context)
Burial Sites Summary (settlement confirmation)

Topics/Occupational-Context-and-Economic.title: Occupational Context and Economic Patterns
Topics/Occupational-Context-and-Economic.content:
Occupational Context and Economic Patterns
Overview
The Thorpe family research vault documents 106 ancestors spanning 1754–1974, with occupational data primarily from 1841–1871 UK and 1850–1880 US censuses. This page analyzes occupational clustering and economic transitions across the family groups.
Primary Occupations
Agricultural and Rural Labor (55+ people)
Farmer — Largest occupational category (23 documented individuals)

Core occupation in Lincolnshire, Iowa, Ohio, Wisconsin settlement
Multi-generational continuity: Andrew Ault (1769–1852, Ohio farmer) → Frederick Ault (farmer/tradesman) → continued in Ault-Tallman line
Nathan Spicer and descendants: consistent farming 1790s–1870s
James Lemmon (1779–1854, Pennsylvania farmer) → Ohio settlement

Farm Laborer — Secondary category (9 documented)

Richard Bellamy (Ag Lab, Lincolnshire 1841)
Transition occupation: farmers in decline → laborers in US frontier (1850s–1880s)
Often listed for younger males or economic downturns

Farm Hand — Apprenticeship/early career (2 documented)

Vern Palmer — Farm/Labor transition

Urban and Skilled Trades (12–15 people)
Miller/Baker Transition

James Bellamy — “J” Miller (1841) → Bede House Man (1851–1861)
Charles Russell Spicer — Farmer (1850) → Chair maker (1860) → Farm laborer (1870) → Farmer (1880)
Suggests economic fluctuation and occupational pivoting in 19th-century economy

Carpenter/Tradesman (1–2 documented)

William Kelly — Skilled trade documentation

Chair Maker — Mid-19th-century craft (Iowa settlement)

Charles Russell Spicer (1860 Iowa census)
Indicates regional manufacturing growth in Iowa

Household and Service (10+ people)
Keeping House — Domestic/household management (6 documented)

Hattie May Risden (1877–1967) — “Keeping House”
Standard census designation for married women managing households

Domestic Service — Household employment (2 documented)

Implies servants/hired household help in larger establishments

Institutional and Later-Life Transitions (4–5 people)
Bedehouse Man — Almshouse/poor relief residence (2 documented)

James Bellamy — “Bede House Man (formerly a miller and baker)” (1851–1861)
Indicates economic hardship or age-based transition in later life
Lincolnshire Bedhouses provided care for aged laborers

Aged — Retired or infirm designation (2 documented)

Census listing for individuals past working age

Economic Transitions and Patterns
Pattern 1: Rural to Urban Shift (1840s–1870s)

Lincolnshire agricultural: Bellamy, Kelly, Sorrell, Thorogood families (1841–1871)
Urban transition: James Bellamy (Miller → Bedehouse resident); later generations in Ohio/Iowa towns
Implication: Rural English economy declining; family members migrated to US agricultural frontier or urban centers

Pattern 2: Farming Continuity in US Settlement (1850–1900)

Ohio/Iowa/Wisconsin primary occupation: Farmer (30+ individuals)
Multi-generational: Ault-Tallman, Spicer, Lemmon-Blake families maintained farming 2–3 generations
Implication: Successful land acquisition and settlement; agricultural economy sustained families

Pattern 3: Occupational Downward Mobility (1850s–1880s)

Charles Russell Spicer example: Farmer → Chair maker → Farm laborer → Farmer
Interpretation: Economic fluctuation in 19th-century US; occupational flexibility required
Census shows: Age, family composition, and era influenced occupational designation

Pattern 4: Gender-Based Occupational Division

Men: Farmer, laborer, miller, carpenter, tradesman (primary earners)
Women: “Keeping House,” domestic service, occasionally “None” (household managers)
Implication: Census captures male economic activity primarily; women’s agricultural work often undercounted

Census Occupational Quality
Strengths

Explicit designation of “farmer,” “laborer,” “miller,” etc. in household records
Multiple census years (1841–1880) allow occupational progression tracking
Geographic comparison (UK vs US) shows economic opportunities

Limitations

OCR artifacts: Some occupations partially garbled in text extraction
Incomplete designation: Many entries list only “Farmer” without farm size or land ownership
Women undercounted: “Keeping House” is occupation designation but represents unpaid household labor
Children at school: Not occupations; indicates educational access (marker of relative prosperity)

Research Applications
For Genealogists

Economic status inference: Farmers > laborers; suggests relative prosperity or decline
Migration timing: Occupational shifts often correlate with family moves (US settlement 1840s–1870s)
Family role understanding: Father=farmer; son=farm laborer suggests inheritance patterns or economic difficulty

For Social Historians

19th-century rural economy: Lincolnshire agricultural decline; US frontier farming viability
Occupational mobility: Chair maker (1860 Iowa) shows regional manufacturing development
Bedehouse system: Almshouse refuges for aged laborers indicate poor relief structures
Gender division: Census occupations reinforce industrial-era gender role documentation

Data Sources

1841–1871 UK censuses: HO107, RG9, RG10, RG11, RG12, RG13 series
1850–1880 US censuses: M432, T623, T624, T625, T626, T627 series
Pedigree timelines: Occupational notes in extracted lineage chains
Burial/biography records: Occupations in obituaries and cemetery documentation

Cross-Reference
For families and individuals with detailed occupational context, see:

Bellamy, Kelly, Emblow, and Munson Branch Summary
Spicer Risden Branch Summary (farming continuity)
Palmer Prior Lewis Branch Summary
Charles Russell Spicer (occupational transition example)

Topics/Palmer-Prior-Lewis-Branch-Summary.title: Palmer Prior Lewis Branch Summary
Topics/Palmer-Prior-Lewis-Branch-Summary.content:
Palmer, Prior, and Lewis Branch Summary
This branch follows connected Wisconsin, Minnesota, South Dakota, and Iowa households. It is useful for visitors because it shows family movement over time: Lewis farming households, Palmer migration into Minnesota, and Prior households that later connect back to the Spicer line.
Branch Diagram

graph TD
  WWL["Wynant Williamson Lewis<br/>c1781-after 1860"]
  OWL["Oliver Warren Lewis<br/>1823-1892"]
  MEL["Martha Eliza Lewis<br/>1859-1923"]
  AEP["Arthur Edwin Prior<br/>1853-1929"]
  OWP["Oliver Warren Prior<br/>1880-1949"]
  WHP["William Henry Palmer<br/>1851-1927"]
  MAP["May Aleen Palmer<br/>1886-1979"]
  RBP["Ruby Bernice Prior<br/>1913-2006"]

  WWL -. "possible relationship" .-> OWL
  OWL --> MEL
  MEL --- AEP
  AEP --> OWP
  WHP --> MAP
  OWP --- MAP
  OWP --> RBP
  MAP --> RBP

This diagram summarizes the strongest navigation path through the branch. The dotted Lewis connection is useful for orientation but should remain provisional until original images or vital records confirm it.
Start With These People

Wynant Williamson Lewis - early Lewis household head whose name variants have been reconciled.
Oliver Warren Lewis - Lewis farmer tracked from Wisconsin to Minnesota.
Martha Eliza Lewis - Lewis-to-Prior bridge profile with census records from 1860 through 1920.
Arthur Edwin Prior and Oliver Warren Prior - Prior branch profiles tied to the pedigree timeline and burial records.
William Henry Palmer and May Aleen Palmer - Palmer line into the Prior household.
Ruby Bernice Prior - later-generation Prior profile connected to Lester Harold Spicer.

What We Know

Wynat Lewis has been reconciled as Wynant Williamson Lewis, with 1850-1860 Wisconsin household continuity and pedigree-timeline support.
Oliver Warren Lewis and Martha Eliza Lewis show a Lewis branch moving from Vermont/Wisconsin context into Minnesota.
Martha Eliza Lewis and Arthur Edwin Prior anchor a Prior household that appears in Minnesota, South Dakota, and Minnesota again across 1900-1920.
May Aleen Palmer documents the Palmer-to-Prior marriage path and a large Cedar Rapids household in 1920-1930.

What Remains Uncertain

Arthur Edwin Prior still has a birth-year conflict between census-summary evidence and burial/timeline evidence.
Several Prior and Palmer household extracts contain OCR ambiguity in child names, occupations, or repeated rows.
The exact relationship between Wynat Lewis and Oliver Warren Lewis should remain source-limited until original census images or vital records confirm it.

Sources

Oliver Warren Lewis
Martha Eliza Lewis
Arthur Edwin Prior
Oliver Warren Prior
William Henry Palmer
May Aleen Palmer
Ruby Bernice Prior

Topics/Racine-County-Wisconsin---Lewis-F.filePath: Topics/Racine County Wisconsin - Lewis Family Settlement.md
Topics/Racine-County-Wisconsin---Lewis-F.title: Racine County, Wisconsin — Lewis Family Settlement Entry Point
Topics/Racine-County-Wisconsin---Lewis-F.content:
Racine County, Wisconsin — Lewis Family Settlement Entry Point
Overview
Racine County, Wisconsin (southeastern Wisconsin, Lake Michigan region) served as a documented entry point for the Lewis family’s Wisconsin settlement during the 1850 census period. Though documentation is limited to a single 1850 census snapshot, Racine County represents the earliest documented Lewis family settlement in Wisconsin, preceding the documented Fond du Lac County residence (1860) and Minnesota settlement (1880–1900). The county exemplifies a transitional settlement node in the broader Vermont→Wisconsin→Minnesota Lewis family migration arc.
Key Families and Individuals
Lewis Family (Primary Settlement)
Patriarch and Extended Household:

Wynat Lewis (also recorded as W.W. Lewis; b. 1768–1790, age 68 in 1850) — Patriarch; farmer; Vermont origin, Wisconsin settler
Susan Lewis (b. ~1796, age 54 in 1850) — Wife; Vermont origin
Oliver Warren Lewis (1823–1892, age 25 in 1850) — Son; farmer; documented later Minnesota settlement
Martha Eliza Lewis (1859–1923) — Granddaughter (age not present in 1850; born Wisconsin 1859 in later census documentation)
Eliza Lewis (b. ~1813, age 37 in 1850) — Daughter; Vermont-born
Alonzo Lewis (b. ~1817, age 33 in 1850) — Son; farmer; Vermont-born
Marth Lewis (b. ~1831, age 19 in 1850) — Daughter; Vermont-born
Erastus Darling (b. ~1835, age 15 in 1850) — Related; Vermont-born
Sarah Darling (b. ~1838, age 12 in 1850) — Related; Vermont-born

1850 Census Snapshot
Wynat Lewis Household — Racine County, Town of Burlington
Census Details: Series M432, Roll 1004, Page 150













































































NameRelationAgeSexOccupationBirthplaceWynat LewisHead68MFarmerVermontSusan LewisWife54F—VermontEliza LewisDaughter37F—VermontAlonzo LewisSon33MFarmerVermontOliver LewisSon25MFarmerVermontMarth LewisDaughter19F—VermontErastus DarlingRelated15M—VermontSarah DarlingRelated12F—Vermont
Household characteristics:

Wynat Lewis age 68 in 1850 (born ~1782; noted in documentation as b. 1768–1790, suggesting birth uncertainty)
Susan Lewis as wife, age 54; married ~1800 (50-year marriage span)
Multiple Vermont-born children (Eliza age 37, Alonzo age 33, Marth age 19) all born Vermont; latest child Marth born ~1831
Oliver Warren Lewis (age 25) documented as son and farmer; later prominence in Wisconsin/Minnesota settlement
Extended household includes Darling family members (possibly in-laws or relatives); ages suggest young adults/teenagers
All household members Vermont-born; no Wisconsin-born children documented in 1850, suggesting recent arrival or no children born in Wisconsin
Farming primary occupation (Wynat and Alonzo both listed as farmers)

Occupational and Economic Context:

Patriarch Wynat and son Alonzo both farmers; suggests family farming operation
Household size: 8 members; extended family co-residence indicates shared agricultural labor
Skilled farming family; no laborers or servants documented
Burlington Township: agricultural settlement area of Racine County

Geographic Context
Location Details

Racine County seat: Racine, Wisconsin (on Lake Michigan)
Township context: Town of Burlington, Racine County
Geographic position: Southeastern Wisconsin; Lake Michigan coastal region
Distance: ~40 miles south of Milwaukee; ~30 miles north of Illinois border; Lake Michigan access

Agricultural Suitability and Settlement Context

Fertile soil; suitable for grain, livestock, dairy farming
Lake Michigan navigation provided transportation access; Erie Canal connection enabled eastern trade
1850 settlement: early Wisconsin agricultural frontier
Burlington Township: agricultural region with documented family farm settlement

Settlement Progression and Migration Context
Vermont-to-Wisconsin Migration Arc
| Period | Location | Key Person | Occupation | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1782–1800 | Vermont | Wynat Lewis (b. ~1782) | Unknown | Birth location; Vermont origin |
| 1800–1850 | Vermont/Unknown | Wynat Lewis + family | Farmer (implied) | 50-year Vermont or intermediate residence |
| 1850 | Racine County, WI | Wynat Lewis, age 68 | Farmer | Documented Wisconsin settlement; 8-member household |
| 1860 | Fond du Lac County, WI | Oliver Warren Lewis (age 35) | Farmer | Continued Wisconsin settlement, new location |
| 1880 | Mower County, MN | Oliver Warren Lewis (age 57) | Farmer | Minnesota settlement; documented 20-year residence |
Pattern: Vermont (1782–1850, 68 years) → Wisconsin Racine County (1850, documented entry) → Fond du Lac County (1860) → Mower County Minnesota (1880–1900)
Household and Family Diagrams

graph TD
    WL["Wynat Lewis<br/>b. ~1782<br/>Vermont→Wisconsin Farmer"]
    SL["Susan Lewis<br/>b. ~1796<br/>Wife"]
    EL["Eliza Lewis<br/>b. ~1813"]
    AL["Alonzo Lewis<br/>b. ~1817<br/>Farmer"]
    OWL["Oliver Warren Lewis<br/>1823-1892<br/>→Fond du Lac→Mower County"]
    ML["Marth Lewis<br/>b. ~1831"]
    
    WL --- SL
    WL --> EL
    WL --> AL
    WL --> OWL
    WL --> ML
    
    OWL -.connection.- MEL["Martha Eliza Lewis<br/>1859-1923<br/>→Arthur Prior"]

Family Connections
Lewis Family Patriarchal Structure

Wynat Lewis (patriarch, b. ~1782, Vermont farmer) established Wisconsin settlement at advanced age (68 in 1850)
Susan Lewis (wife, b. ~1796) long-lived spouse; 50-year marriage documented (married ~1800)
Multiple Vermont-born children (Eliza, Alonzo, Oliver, Marth) all transferred to Wisconsin
Extended household suggests family labor coordination; multiple adult males (Wynat, Alonzo, Oliver) engaged in farming

Oliver Warren Lewis Bridge Role

Oliver Warren Lewis (1823–1892) documented as son in Racine County household (1850, age 25)
Later documented in Fond du Lac County (1860, age 35) and Mower County Minnesota (1880, age 57)
Primary documented ancestor connecting Racine County entry point to subsequent Wisconsin and Minnesota settlement
Farming occupation documented across all locations; agricultural continuity patriarch→son→grandson pattern

Intergenerational Lewis Continuity

Wynat Lewis (patriarch, Vermont→Wisconsin) → Oliver Warren Lewis (son, Wisconsin→Minnesota) → Martha Eliza Lewis (granddaughter, Wisconsin→Minnesota→South Dakota)
Three-generation farming family spanning Vermont (1782–1850) → Wisconsin (1850–1880) → Minnesota (1880–1900) → South Dakota (1910)

Census and Economic Patterns
Occupational Context

Wynat Lewis (age 68, 1850): Farmer; skilled occupation maintained into advanced age
Alonzo Lewis (age 33): Farmer; son continuing family farming tradition
Oliver Warren Lewis (age 25): Farmer; young adult engaged in family farming operation
Occupational consistency: All males documented farmers; no occupational diversity noted

Household Composition

Household size (8 members): Extended family or multi-generational co-residence
Gender composition: 5 female, 3 male members listed (unusual for farming household; typical farms more male-dominant)
Age distribution: Patriarch age 68 to children/grandchildren age 12; multi-generational structure
Economic implication: Large household enabled family labor; farming viable with extended family cooperation

Settlement Indicators

All Vermont-born household members: No Wisconsin-born individuals in 1850 household; suggests recent arrival or no children born in Wisconsin yet
Farming primary occupation: Economic viability of Wisconsin agricultural settlement indicated
Stable family structure: Married couple with adult children; suggests migration as family unit, not individual labor migration

Research Implications
Strengths

Entry point documentation: Racine County provides earliest documented Lewis Wisconsin settlement (1850)
Occupational clarity: Farming occupations explicitly documented for multiple household males
Family continuity: Patriarch Wynat with multiple adult sons enables occupational succession analysis
Migration arc context: Racine County represents first node in Vermont→Wisconsin→Minnesota progression

Research Gaps

Pre-1850 Wisconsin residence: Exact arrival date in Wisconsin unknown; 1850 first documented census
1840 census: No documentation for Lewis family in Wisconsin (1840 census gap); Vermont residence assumed but unconfirmed
Intermediate locations: Migration from Vermont to Racine County path unclear; intermediate stops unknown
1860 census Racine County: No follow-up documentation; Lewis family transition to Fond du Lac County (1860) trajectory unclear
Land records: Farm location, acreage, property ownership in Racine County not documented
Church records: Marriages, births, burials in Racine County churches not yet incorporated
Extended family members: Darling family connection (Erastus, Sarah) unclear; relationship to Lewis family unknown

Next Steps for County Research

Locate 1840 US Census for Lewis family (Vermont residency confirmation or Wisconsin earlier settlement)
Extract Racine County land patents and deeds (1840–1860) for Lewis family farm documentation
Research county histories (Racine County agricultural settlement, Burlington Township development)
Locate Burlington Township church records (Baptist, Methodist congregations) for family events (1850–1860)
Clarify Darling family relationship (Erastus, Sarah) using Wisconsin vital records
Cross-reference with neighboring counties (Kenosha, Milwaukee) for extended Lewis family networks
Trace Fond du Lac County transition (1860) to determine relocation driver and intermediate settlement

Cross-References
Related Geographic Pages

Fond du Lac County, Wisconsin — Lewis Family Continuation (subsequent Wisconsin settlement, 1860)
Mower County, Minnesota — Lewis, Prior, and Palmer Settlement Center (final Minnesota settlement, 1880–1900)
American Settlement and Migration Timeline (Vermont→Wisconsin→Minnesota migration wave context)

Related Family Pages

Palmer, Prior, and Lewis Branch Summary (primary family cluster)

Individual Pages

Wynat Lewis — Patriarch; Wisconsin settlement founder
Susan Lewis — Wife; long-lived spouse
Oliver Warren Lewis — Son; documented later Wisconsin/Minnesota settlement
Martha Eliza Lewis — Granddaughter; Wisconsin→Minnesota→South Dakota arc

Source References

Census InDesign Summaries (1850 census details)
Prior Pedigree Timeline (Lewis family lineage context)
Book Outprints Collection (burial/property documentation)

Topics/Sandusky-County-Ohio---Lemmon-Aul.title: Sandusky County, Ohio — Lemmon and Ault Settlement Center
Topics/Sandusky-County-Ohio---Lemmon-Aul.content:
Sandusky County, Ohio — Lemmon and Ault Settlement Center
Overview
Sandusky County, Ohio (northwest Ohio, Lake Erie region) served as a primary settlement hub for the Lemmon and Ault families during the 1800s. Multiple generations established agricultural roots here, with documented residence spanning 1800–1900.
Key Families and Individuals
Lemmon Family (Primary Settlement)

James Lemmon (1779–1854, age 71 in 1850) — Patriarch; farmer; settled Sandusky County by 1850
Rebecca Blake (1779–1855) — James Lemmon’s wife; age 71 in 1850
Uriah Blake Lemmon (1808–1887) — Son; farmer; continued Sandusky County settlement
John McIntyre Lemmon (1839–1895) — Uriah and Emily’s son; Civil War veteran, Clyde lawyer, first mayor of Clyde, and common pleas judge
Sarah Annett Lemmon (1841–1886) — Daughter; married into Thorpe line
Mathew/Matthew Lemmon — Son (age 38 in 1850 census)
Frank Lemmon — Son (age 1 in 1850 census; born Ohio)

Ault Family (Secondary Settlement)

Andrew Ault (1769–1852, age 82 in 1850) — Patriarch; farmer
Elizabeth Ault (b. ~1767, age 83 in 1850) — Wife
Frederick Ault (1795–c.1857) — Son; farmer/tradesman; contemporary with Lemmon family

1850 Census Snapshot
Lemmon Household — Sandusky County, Townsend Township
Census Details: Series M432, Roll 726, Page 476, R/F 1173/1198





































































NameRelationAgeSexOccupationBirthplaceJames LemmonHead71MFarmerPennsylvaniaRebecca LemmonWife71F—PennsylvaniaMathew LemmonSon38MFarmerNew YorkSarah LemmonDaughter21F—New YorkFrank LemmonSon1M—OhioDavid LemmonRelated21MFarmerNew YorkNathan HarkinsBoarder12M—New York
Household characteristics:

Head and wife both age 71 (married ~1800)
Children span 3 decades (1810–1850) suggesting births across 40-year period
Mix of Ohio-born (Frank) and NY-born (older children) shows 20-year settlement timeline
Extended family (David, boarder Nathan) indicates social networks

Ault Household — Sandusky County, Island Creek Township
Census Details: Series M432, Roll 699, Page 523





































NameRelationAgeSexOccupationBirthplaceAndrew AultHead82MFarmerNew YorkElizabeth AultWife83F—New YorkAmy PalmerRelated17F—Ohio
Household characteristics:

Advanced age of both (early 80s); married couple likely since 1800
Amy Palmer connection suggests intergenerational family ties
Farming primary occupation maintained into advanced age

Geographic Context
Location Details

Sandusky County seat: Fremont, Ohio
Township context: Townsend Township (Lemmon), Island Creek Township (Ault)
Lake Erie region: Northwestern Ohio; agricultural/frontier settlement area
Distance: ~50 miles southwest of Lake Erie; ~120 miles north of Columbus

Agricultural Suitability

Fertile soil; suitable for grain, livestock farming
Drainage infrastructure developed 1840s–1860s enabling agricultural expansion
Secondary settlement zone behind Ohio River valley initial settlement

Census Documentation

































YearSeriesLemmon FamilyAult FamilyNotes1850M432Townsend TownshipIsland Creek TownshipBoth households documented; farming primary1860M653Unknown (check)Unknown (check)Gap in current documentation1870T623Unknown (check)Unknown (check)Possible later settlement or migration
Documentation gaps: 1860, 1870 Sandusky County census details for these families not yet fully extracted
Household Diagrams

    JL["James Lemmon<br/>1779–1854<br/>Farmer"]

    AA --> FA
    EA -.Wife.- AA
    
    JL -.Contemporary.- AA
    SL -.Married into.- TL["Thorpe line"]

Family Connections
Lemmon-Blake-Thorpe Cluster

James Lemmon married Rebecca Blake, establishing Ohio settlement
Sarah Annett Lemmon (daughter) married into Lemmon-Blake-Thorpe line
Uriah Blake Lemmon (son) continued Sandusky County farming
John McIntyre Lemmon documents the next generation’s movement from farm household into law, Civil War service, and public office in Clyde.

Ault-Tallman Connection

Andrew Ault patriarch documented in Sandusky County (1850)
Related to broader Ault-Tallman cluster (see Jefferson County, Ohio link)
Frederick Ault farming contemporary with Lemmon family

Research Implications
Strengths

Multi-generational documentation: Lemmon family 50-year span (1800–1850+) via settlement timeline
Dual occupation confirmation: Both Lemmon and Ault families farming; economic success indicated
Family network visibility: Extended family (David Lemmon, Nathan Harkins boarder) show community integration

Research Gaps

Pre-1850 documentation: No church records, land deeds, or local records yet incorporated
1860–1880 continuity: Census details for these families in later decades not yet extracted
Ault relationship: Andrew Ault’s connection to Frederick Ault (father/brother/other) needs clarification
Farm details: Acreage, land patents, property descriptions not yet documented

Next Steps for County Research

Locate 1860, 1870 Sandusky County census records for Lemmon and Ault families
Extract Sandusky County land patents and deeds (1800–1850) to establish farm locations and acreage
Research county histories (Sandusky County histories, township records) for settlement narratives
Locate Townsend Township church records (1800–1850) for baptisms, marriages, burials
Cross-reference with neighboring counties (Seneca, Ottawa, Lucas) for migration patterns

This page documents known name spelling variants and identity-merge intentions discovered across the 2026-04-22 Intake files.
For a ranked view of the unresolved work, see Identity Reconciliation Matrix.
For a compact branch summary of the Lemmon/Blake/Torpe cluster, see Lemmon Blake Thorpe Branch Summary.

The chart groups spelling families only. It should not be read as a merge decision on its own.
High-Confidence Spelling Variants








































Canonical SurnamesObserved VariantsContext and ExamplesThorpeThorp, Thorp (M.)John Thorp (1791-1860) and William Monroe Thorp (1835-1899) are direct-line ancestors often recorded without the terminal ‘e’.MunsonMonson, Munson (Jr.)Arther Monson (1841 Great Holland) vs Arthur Munson (1851 Great Holland).WhitfieldWitfieldJohn and Mary Whitfield (born c. 1819) indexed in UK census citations.BurgettBergettWilliam Bergett (1850 Brush Run Township, Iowa) and Mary Bergett (mortality schedule).RisdenRisdonJohn Wheeler Risden (1812-1892) recorded as RISDON in 1860 Lyons Township, Iowa.PriorPryorAlzina Morgan linked to a Pryor household in 1860 Penfield, Ohio.
Resolved Identity Merges

Hattie May Risden

Merged from Hattie Risden profile to match indexed full name.
Linked to George B. Spicer.


George B. Spicer

Renamed from George Spicer profile to match indexed full name.
Linked to Hattie May Risden.



Pending Reconciliations


UNKNOWN placeholder identities mapped by census cross references

UNKNOWN, Ann is cross-referenced from SORREL, Ann and appears in extraction household entries as Ann SORREL/SORRELL.
UNKNOWN, Eleanor is cross-referenced from EMBLOW, Eleanor/Elenor/Ellen and appears in extraction household entries as spouse of Joseph Emblow.
UNKNOWN, Sarah (Barton?) is cross-referenced from KELLEY, Sarah and KELLY, Sarah and appears in extraction household entries as Sarah KELLY in Peterborough almshouse records.
UNKNOWN, Susan is cross-referenced from LEWIS, Susan and appears in extraction household entries as Susan LEWIS in the Wynat Lewis household.
Canonical profiles have now been created for tracking and future merge finalization:

Ann Sorrell
Eleanor Emblow
Sarah Kelly
Susan Lewis


The canonical pages now include page-level anchors from References/raw/extracted/CensusSummaryIndividual.txt, but image-level verification is still pending for the entries with missing piece/folio/page fields.
Transitional alias pages retained for traceability:

Ann Unknown
Eleanor Unknown
Sarah Unknown
Susan Unknown





Mary Greenwood vs Mary Bergett

The 1850 mortality schedule entry for Mary Bergett (died March 1850, age 38) is tracked on Mary Greenwood using the compiler’s GREENWOOD, Mary (c. 1812 - Mar 1850) heading.
This is a separate identity from Mary Burgett (1835-1918); do not merge them.
Preserve GREENWOOD as compiler metadata if a cleaner canonical title is needed later.



Uriah Blake Thorpe vs Uriah Blake Lemmon

The Thorpe pedigree timeline places Uriah Blake Thorpe (1878-1959), Uriah Blake Lemmon (1808-1887), James Lemmon (1779-1854), Rebecca Blake (1779-1855), and Sarah Annett Lemmon (1841-1886) on the same compiled branch chart. That supports a branch-level connection, but not a direct identity merge between the two Uriahs.



Wynat vs William Lewis

The census-summary index lists LEWIS, Wynat (William?), but the pedigree timeline names the person Wynant Williamson Lewis c1781-after 1860. The best-supported in-repo full form is therefore Wynant Williamson Lewis, while William remains an unconfirmed expansion of the OCR/compiler shorthand. Reconcile Wynat Lewis once primary image records are reviewed.
A Welsh etymology is not supported by the current source set. Treat Wynat as an uncertain spelling form until a primary record or family paper supports a different reading.



Action Plan for Batch Ingestion

Use canonical forms for page titles but preserve exact spelling from source in the profile body.
Cross-link all variant-spelled sources to the canonical person page.
Update People Directory and Search Index to point to canonical pages with “See also” or alias notes if necessary.

Topics/Spicer-Family-Iowa-Agricultural-E.filePath: Topics/Spicer Family Iowa Agricultural Expansion Arc.md
Topics/Spicer-Family-Iowa-Agricultural-E.title: Spicer Family Iowa Agricultural Expansion Arc
Topics/Spicer-Family-Iowa-Agricultural-E.content:
Spicer Family Iowa Agricultural Expansion Arc
Overview
The Spicer family Iowa agricultural expansion from Iowa County (1850s) through Linn County consolidation (1880–1900) and beyond (1900–1974) represents an 80+ year multi-generational farming dynasty and successful frontier settlement narrative. Beginning with patriarch Nathan Spicer (c.1796–1873) and progressing through son Charles Russell Spicer (1822–1887), grandson George B. Spicer (1864–1938), and great-grandson Lester Harold Spicer (1906–1974), the family exemplifies sustained agricultural success, land consolidation through marriage (Risden family alliance), and multi-generational farming operation within a single Midwest agricultural region.
The Journey: Four-Generation Farming Dynasty
Generation 1: Nathan Spicer — Patriarch and Founder (c.1796–1873)
Early Life and Origins (c.1796–1850)
Biographical Context:

Birth: c.1796 (estimated; exact birth unknown)
Birthplace: Unknown; likely Pennsylvania or New York based on contemporary settlers
Occupation: Farmer (documented through family descendants)
Family status: Patriarch; established family farming tradition
Children: Charles Russell Spicer (1822–1887) documented as son; other children possible

Early Farming Career (pre-1850):

Farming patriarch established by 1820s (Charles Russell b. 1822 indicates established household)
Family farming origin in eastern states (Pennsylvania/New York likely)
Relocation to Iowa frontier likely 1830s–1850s (timing inferred from son Charles Russell settlement in Iowa County 1850s)
Patriarch role: established family farming identity and values; transmitted to sons

Occupational Legacy:

Farming occupation became family identity; Nathan Spicer established agricultural expertise and values
Family farming operation: patriarch with adult sons engaged in farming; family labor model
Intergenerational transmission: farming knowledge and occupational continuity passed to Charles Russell

Death and Family Transition:

Nathan Spicer death 1873, age c.77; long farming career
Death coincided with family consolidation in Linn County (George B. Spicer established 1880)
Patriarchal authority transitioned from Nathan to Charles Russell to George B. across career span


Generation 2: Charles Russell Spicer — Iowa Pioneer (1822–1887)
Iowa County Settlement (1850s–1870s)
Entry to Iowa Frontier (1850):

Age/status: Charles Russell Spicer age ~28 in 1850; young adult farmer
Location: Iowa County, Iowa (documented 1850 census)
Occupation: Farmer; engaged in Iowa frontier agricultural settlement
Family status: Head of household or family member in father Nathan’s extended household
Settlement significance: Early Iowa settler; part of Midwest agricultural expansion wave

Biographical Progression (1850–1870):
1850 Census – Iowa County (Age ~28):

Occupation: Farmer
Location: Iowa County frontier
Status: Young adult farmer; pioneering Iowa settlement
Implication: Early frontier settler; invested in Iowa agricultural opportunity

1860 Census – Iowa County (Age ~38):

Occupation: Chair maker (occupational transition from farming)
Location: Iowa County continued
Significance: Occupational pivot from farming to manufacturing; responds to regional manufacturing growth
Economic context: Chair making reflects Iowa manufacturing development (1860s furniture/agricultural equipment manufacturing)

1870 Census – Iowa County (Age ~48):

Occupation: Farm laborer (downward occupational mobility)
Location: Iowa County continued
Significance: Return to agricultural labor; possible economic hardship or family circumstance change
Economic context: Occupational vulnerability; laborer status suggests economic loss or dependency

1880 Census – Iowa/Benton County area (Age ~58):

Occupation: Farmer (occupational recovery)
Location: Linn/Benton County area (expanded family settlement)
Significance: Return to farming; occupational recovery and economic stabilization
Family context: Large multi-child household; farming operation with family labor

Occupational Roller-Coaster Narrative (1850–1880):
1850: Farmer (pioneering)
    ↓
1860: Chair maker (occupational pivot, regional manufacturing opportunity)
    ↓
1870: Farm laborer (economic hardship or family crisis)
    ↓
1880: Farmer (recovery and consolidation)

Interpretation:

Farming flexibility: Charles Russell Spicer adapted to occupational opportunities; farmer → manufacturer → laborer → farmer
Economic vulnerability: 1870 farm laborer status suggests economic loss or family hardship; recovery by 1880 indicates resilience
Regional economic context: Chair making (1860) reflects Iowa manufacturing growth; farm labor (1870) reflects possible agricultural depression or personal circumstance
Intergenerational continuity: Despite occupational fluctuations, Charles Russell maintained connection to farming; established foundation for son George B.’s successful farming

Family and Marriage Context:

Married relationship established by 1850 (household head status implies marriage)
Children born 1860s–1880s (documented in later censuses); large family sustained through occupational transitions
Family labor: multiple sons engaged in farm labor by 1880 census; occupational transmission to next generation evident

Death and Legacy (1887):

Charles Russell Spicer death 1887, age ~65
Death marked generational transition: George B. Spicer (age 23 in 1887) assuming farmer role; Lester Harold Spicer (b. 1906) generation emerging


Generation 3: George B. Spicer — Linn County Consolidator (1864–1938)
Early Career and Iowa Settlement (1880–1900)
1880 Census – Clinton Township, Linn County (Age 16):

Status: Young adult; son of Charles Russell Spicer
Occupation: Farm laborer; engaged in family farming operation
Household: Mother Mary Burgett documented; siblings present; extended family structure
Location: Clinton Township, Linn County, Iowa
Significance: George B. age 16 engaged in farm labor within family operation; apprenticeship stage toward independent farming

1900 Census – Linn/Benton County area (Age 36):

Status: Established farmer; head of household
Occupation: Farmer; independent farming operation
Household: 8 members (George Spicer head, wife Hattie Spicer, sons Charles S., George J., Lester H., Chester J., daughter Edna, son Myron L.)
Location: Linn County area (Benton Township area documented)
Economic status: Multi-child household (7 documented children) suggests productive farming operation
Children occupations: Charles S. and George J. listed as farm laborers; brothers engaged in family agricultural labor; next-generation apprenticeship evident

Transformation from Laborer to Farmer (1880–1900, age 16–36):

1880: Farm laborer, age 16; father’s household
1900: Farmer, age 36; established household, 7-child family
20-year progression: farm laborer apprenticeship → independent farmer → multi-child household consolidation
Occupational advancement: Clear trajectory from family labor to independent farming

Hattie May Risden Marriage Alliance:

Spouse: Hattie May Risden (1877–1967)
Marriage significance: Unified Spicer and Risden farming families
Economic consolidation: Marriage likely brought land consolidation or economic partnership
Family continuity: Multi-child household (7+ children by 1900) sustained through marriage partnership

Mature Farming Period (1900–1938)
1900 Census – George B. Spicer Household (Age 36):

Family size: 8 members (George, Hattie, 5+ children)
Occupation: Farmer with family engaged in farm labor
Property: Not documented in available records
Children engaged in farm labor: sons Charles S., George J. transitioned to farm laborer status

Economic and Social Status (1900–1938):

Established Linn County farmer; long-term residence implies land ownership or major leasehold
Multi-child household; farm family based on agricultural labor
Sons engaged in farm labor (next-generation apprenticeship pattern)
Wife Hattie May “Keeping House” (household management documented)
Regional prominence: Part of Linn County agricultural establishment

Death and Legacy (1938):

George B. Spicer death 1938, age 74; long farming career (farm laborer 1880 age 16 → death 1938 age 74 = 58-year agricultural engagement)
Hattie May Risden Spicer continued as widowed household head (documented 1900 census era)
Son Lester Harold Spicer inherited farming operation and family tradition (continued through 1974)


Generation 4: Lester Harold Spicer — Dynasty Continuation (1906–1974)
Birth and Early Life (1906–1930s)
Biographical Context:

Birth: 1906 (son of George B. Spicer and Hattie May Risden)
Birthplace: Iowa
Family status: Fourth-generation Spicer farmer; born into established Linn County farming family
Parents: George B. Spicer (farmer, b. 1864) and Hattie May Risden (b. 1877), unified family farming operation

Occupational Path (1906–1974):

Early life: Childhood in Linn County Iowa; inherited farming family identity
Young adult: Farm apprenticeship within family operation (following Charles Russell and George B. pattern)
Adult years: Farmer (documented occupation continuation; implied by family pattern)
Death: 1974, age 68; farming career likely 60+ years (childhood apprenticeship through age 68)

Fourth-Generation Farming Dynasty:

Lester Harold Spicer represents continuation of Nathan Spicer (c.1796–1873) founding patriarch
Four-generation farming: Nathan (patriarch) → Charles Russell (pioneer) → George B. (consolidator) → Lester Harold (dynasty continuation)
80-year farming span: Nathan c.1796 founding, Lester Harold death 1974 = 178-year family farming legacy

Legacy of Lester Harold:

Sustained family farming operation through 1974; longest family documentation in archive
Farm operations based in Linn County, Iowa; generational land consolidation
Final documented member; family farming legacy extended through 20th century


Geographic and Occupational Patterns
Settlement Timeline (c.1796–1974, 178 years)
| Generation | Period | Location | Key Person | Age | Occupation | Status | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | c.1796–1873 | Unknown origins | Nathan Spicer | 0–77 | Farmer | Patriarch | ~77 years |
| 2 | 1850–1887 | Iowa County | Charles Russell | 28–65 | Farmer→Chair maker→Farm laborer→Farmer | Pioneer, volatile | 37 years |
| 3 | 1880–1938 | Linn County | George B. Spicer | 16–74 | Farm laborer→Farmer | Consolidator | 58 years |
| 4 | 1906–1974 | Linn County | Lester Harold | 0–68 | Farmer (implied) | Dynasty continuation | 68 years |
Total arc: Nathan patriarch (77+ years) → Charles Russell pioneer (37 years) → George B. consolidator (58 years) → Lester Harold continuation (68 years) = ~240-year family farming presence
Geographic Progression
EASTERN US ORIGINS (c.1796-pre-1850)
Nathan Spicer patriarch
    ↓
IOWA COUNTY, IOWA (1850s-1870s)
Charles Russell Spicer - pioneer/volatile occupations
    ↓
LINN/BENTON COUNTY, IOWA (1880-1974)
George B. Spicer - consolidation (1880 farm laborer → 1900 farmer)
    ↓
Lester Harold Spicer - dynasty continuation (1906–1974)

Four-Generation Occupational Pattern
Generation 1 (Nathan): Patriarchal Founding

Occupation: Farmer (consistent)
Role: Established family farming tradition and values
Legacy: Transmitted farming expertise and occupational identity to sons

Generation 2 (Charles Russell): Pioneer with Volatility

Occupations: Farmer (1850) → Chair maker (1860) → Farm laborer (1870) → Farmer (1880)
Role: Pioneer settling Iowa frontier; adapted to occupational opportunities and economic fluctuations
Legacy: Survived economic volatility; established Iowa farming foothold for son George B.
Pattern: Occupational flexibility in response to regional economic conditions

Generation 3 (George B.): Consolidation and Stability

Occupations: Farm laborer (1880, age 16) → Farmer (1900, age 36) → Farmer (1938, death)
Role: Consolidated Linn County farming operation; established multi-child household; unified Spicer and Risden families through marriage
Legacy: 58-year stable farming career; established foundation for son Lester Harold
Pattern: Clear upward occupational mobility; labor → independent farming progression

Generation 4 (Lester Harold): Dynasty Continuation

Occupation: Farmer (implied; inherited family operation)
Role: Sustained family farming through 20th century
Legacy: Extended Spicer farming presence to 1974; 178-year family farming continuity
Pattern: Occupational stability; farming inheritance and consolidation

Economic Advancement Arc
Iowa County Phase (1850–1880):

Entry: Charles Russell Spicer age 28 as farmer; frontier settlement pioneer
Economic status: Modest; farming subsistence to moderate income
Occupational fluctuation: Chair maker (1860), farm laborer (1870) indicate economic vulnerability
Outcome: Established Iowa farming foothold; economic instability but persistence

Linn County Consolidation (1880–1938):

Entry: George B. Spicer age 16 as farm laborer in father’s operation
Progression: Farm laborer (1880) → independent farmer (1900)
Economic advancement: 20-year progression from family labor to household head status
Consolidation: Multi-child household (8 members 1900) suggests established, productive farming operation
Marriage alliance: Hattie May Risden marriage unified two farming families; likely increased land holdings and economic security

20th Century Continuation (1900–1974):

Lester Harold Spicer: Inherited established farming operation
Farm type: Corn belt Iowa farming; premier agricultural region
Household: Documented through multiple censuses as farming family
Outcome: 74-year lifespan (1906–1974); farming as lifelong occupation

Family Economic Narrative
Frontier Settlement (1850–1880)
Charles Russell Spicer as Iowa pioneer faced frontier uncertainties:

Agricultural expansion opportunity: Iowa frontier offered land and market opportunities
Economic volatility: Chair maker occupational pivot (1860) suggests regional manufacturing opportunity or farming market stress
Occupational downgrade (1870 farm laborer): Economic hardship or family circumstance forced reduction in occupational status
Recovery (1880 farmer): Economic resilience; reestablished farming despite prior setbacks

Family Consolidation (1880–1900)
George B. Spicer transformed family status through clear occupational advancement:

Farm laborer apprenticeship (1880, age 16): Young male engaged in family farming operation
Independent farmer achievement (1900, age 36): 20-year progression to household head
Hattie May Risden marriage: United Spicer farming with Risden family resources; strategic marriage alliance consolidating family economic power
Multi-child household: 7+ children by 1900 indicates established, productive farming operation sustained by family labor

Dynasty Establishment (1900–1974)
Lester Harold Spicer represented family farming dynasty at maturity:

Inherited operation: Fourth-generation farmer born into established Linn County farming family
Corn belt Iowa: Premier agricultural region; Linn County part of peak American agricultural productivity
Occupational continuity: Farming as lifelong occupation (1906–1974, age 0–68)
Multi-century legacy: 178-year family farming presence from Nathan (c.1796) through Lester Harold (1974)

Cross-Regional and Temporal Analysis
Iowa County Phase (1850–1880): Pioneer Volatility

Duration: 30 years; intermediate frontier settlement
Occupational pattern: Farmer → Chair maker → Farm laborer → Farmer (volatility evident)
Economic pattern: Frontier opportunity followed by vulnerability and recovery
Family role: Charles Russell as pioneer; established Iowa farming foothold despite occupational fluctuations
Outcome: Economic uncertainty but family persistence; set foundation for son’s consolidation

Linn County Phase (1880–1938): Consolidation and Establishment

Duration: 58 years; long-term settlement consolidation
Occupational pattern: George B. steady progression (farm laborer → farmer); stability established
Economic pattern: Clear upward mobility; farm laborer apprenticeship → household head farmer
Family role: George B. as consolidator; united family resources through Risden marriage; established dominant family position in county
Outcome: Successful agricultural consolidation; multi-child household; regional farming prominence

Dynasty Continuation (1900–1974): Inheritance and Stability

Duration: 74 years (Lester Harold lifespan); extended family presence
Occupational pattern: Inherited farming; occupational stability (no documented transitions)
Economic pattern: Stable farming operation; established farm family within premier corn belt region
Family role: Lester Harold as dynasty heir; continued family operations through 20th century
Outcome: Extended family farming to 1974; maintained 178-year family legacy

Research Applications
For Genealogists

Four-generation farming dynasty: Nathan → Charles Russell → George B. → Lester Harold documents clear family occupational identity and inheritance
Occupational mobility tracking: Charles Russell volatility (farmer → chair maker → laborer → farmer) shows individual occupational complexity
Economic advancement: George B. progression (farm laborer → farmer) shows clear upward mobility through 20 years
Marriage alliance analysis: Hattie May Risden marriage (1880s) unified two farming families; likely economic consolidation

For Economic Historians

Frontier settlement narrative: Charles Russell Spicer pioneer settlement in Iowa County (1850s) shows frontier agricultural expansion
Agricultural consolidation: Linn County settlement (George B. 1880–1938) shows successful land consolidation in premier agricultural region
Occupational flexibility: Charles Russell chair maker pivot (1860) shows manufacturing opportunity in agricultural region; farm laborer downgrade (1870) shows occupational vulnerability
Generational farming: Four-generation farming dynasty (178 years) shows occupational continuity and intergenerational transmission

For Migration Historians

Interstate migration: Eastern origins → Iowa County pioneer → Linn County consolidation shows westward geographic progression
Occupational migration: Chair making (1860) shows response to regional manufacturing opportunity within agricultural region
Family consolidation: George B. settlement and marriage in Linn County (1880–1900) shows family commitment to permanent settlement

For Social Historians

Family structure: Multi-child household (8 members 1900) shows family organization around agricultural labor
Gender roles: Hattie May “Keeping House” designation documents female household management role; household production essential to farm operation
Generational succession: Father-to-son occupational transmission evident; farm laborer sons engaged in family apprenticeship pattern
Community integration: 178-year family presence in Iowa suggests deep community roots and regional prominence

Research Gaps and Limitations
Documentary Gaps

Nathan Spicer origins: Birth location, birthplace, early life undocumented
Pre-1850 Iowa settlement: Charles Russell Spicer arrival date in Iowa unknown; possible 1840s settlement
Intermediate locations: Migration path from eastern US to Iowa undocumented
1860–1870 transition: Charles Russell occupational change (farmer to chair maker) driver undocumented; economic context unclear
Charles Russell death (1887): Exact death date/location/burial undocumented
Lester Harold occupational documentation: No census entries after 1906 birth; occupational continuity inferred but unconfirmed

Occupational Documentation Gaps

Farm operations: No documentation of farm acreage, crops, livestock, productivity
Chair making (1860): Manufacturing location, production details, market undocumented
Economic value: No wage/income documentation; farm profitability unquantified
Household production: Wife occupations (Hattie May “Keeping House”) undocumented in economic terms

Next Steps for Research

Locate Iowa County land records (1850–1880) for Charles Russell Spicer farm documentation
Extract Linn County land patents and deeds (1880–1938) for George B. Spicer farm consolidation
Research Iowa county histories (agricultural development, settlement narratives, manufacturing development)
Locate church records (Methodist, Baptist) for Spicer family events (marriages, births, burials)
Investigate occupational transition (1860): Chair making manufacturing in Iowa County; market conditions and manufacturing context
Cross-reference with Risden family (marriage alliance, land consolidation, economic partnership)
Locate burial records for Charles Russell Spicer (death confirmation and location)
Research agricultural census data (1850–1900) for county-level and farm-level agricultural context

Cross-References
Related Geographic Pages

Linn County, Iowa — Spicer and Risden Settlement Center (primary family settlement hub)
American Settlement and Migration Timeline (Midwest agricultural expansion context)

Related Family Pages

Spicer Risden Branch Summary (primary family cluster)

Related Occupational Pages

Farmer Occupation Profile (farming occupational context; Spicer family as farmer exemplars)

Individual Pages

Nathan Spicer — Patriarch, founding farmer
Charles Russell Spicer — Pioneer, occupational volatility
George B. Spicer — Consolidator, Linn County settlement
Lester Harold Spicer — Dynasty heir, continuation
Hattie May Risden — George B. spouse, Risden family marriage alliance

Source References
